EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported trees down in Rivesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic air moved into West Virginia during the early morning on the 22nd.  Temperatures dropped into the minus 5 to plus 5 degree range by dawn, then rose only slowly by late morning.   Wind chill readings were mostly in the minus 10 to minus 15 degree range, with some minus 20 degrees across mountain ridges.  County school districts started the day on a 2 hour delay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving northeastward across the forecast area brought a combination of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain during the day on the 14th. Strong isentropic lift brought the highest snow amounts to areas along and north of I-80, and in the higher elevations, where cold air remained entrenched the longest. A dry slot brought an end to the heaviest snow in the afternoon Saturday. Snow totals generally were under 6 inches in 12 hours except in the ridges of West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air crossing the Great Lakes in northwest flow produced widespread snow across western Pennsylvania, and the higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from the evening of the 23rd into early afternoon of the 24th. Very heavy snow fell across Venango and Clarion counties in Pennsylvania as a nearly stationary band of snow developed from Lake Huron across Lake Erie with 8-20 inches of snow across northern Venango county, and 6 to 12 inches across portions of Clarion county. Outside of this heavier band 2 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges, and across northern portions of western Pennsylvania. 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and in Garrett county Maryland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed along a cold front across Ohio the evening of October 31st. This line moved east during the overnight hours of November 1st and was able to mix strong winds aloft down to the surface. Severe thunderstorm wind damage was reported across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. At one time nearly 50 thousand homes were without power across the region due to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported multiple trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of November 12th producing a widespread snowfall. A general 1 to 3 inches of snow fell in a 6 to 8 hour period behind the cold front across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. In the higher elevations of Preston, Tucker, and Garrett counties up to 4 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across northern West Virginia, with around 4 inches of snow at Canaan Valley in Tucker county, and 4 inches of snow at Rowlesburg in Preston county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed ahead of and along a cold front that moved southeast from the eastern Great Lakes across the Upper Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large number of trees were reported down on US-250.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist air mass for early September helped scattered thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon and evening. Isolated flooding and wind damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported trees down on East Cove Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist air mass for early September helped scattered thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon and evening. Isolated flooding and wind damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported trees down on Castleman Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More scattered showers and thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An adult male was outside when a bolt of lightning indirectly hit his leg after traveling across some concrete.  He was taken to the hospital and stayed overnight for observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More scattered showers and thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was struck on Wolfpen Lane, but refused any treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold moist air rising over the mountains caused snow to break out for locations along and west of the Allegheny front in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 3.5 inches near Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy downpours caused flooding across portions of southeastern Marion county in West Virginia early in the morning on August 13th. Several roads were closed.
Heavy downpours caused flooding across portions of southeastern Marion county in West Virginia early in the morning on August 13th. Several roads were closed.

An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was located over the Atlantic and tropical moisture was drawn into the Mid Atlantic. Clearing west of the Blue Ridge increased instability and thunderstorms developed with ample moisture to produce heavy rain and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down across Nethken Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was located over the Atlantic and tropical moisture was drawn into the Mid Atlantic. Clearing west of the Blue Ridge increased instability and thunderstorms developed with ample moisture to produce heavy rain and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down on Ford Hill Road south of Rio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing cold front swept through during the evening of the 16th.  The coverage and intensity of the snow showers increased, especially in the central mountains.  The snow showers diminished after dawn on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were common in the central mountains.  Temperatures fell into the single digits across the high terrain for the morning of the 17th.

A low pressure system moving towards the Great Lakes helped to push a strong cold front across the Ohio Valley in the early morning hours of the 22nd. A shallow convective line of thunderstorms developed along the front, providing for some strong to severe wind gusts. Damage was reported in several locations across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local fire department reported scattered trees down around the area.

After a one day lull in the active weather on Saturday the 7th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain quickly spread back northeast into West Virginia by dawn on Sunday the 8th.||Strong low level warm air advection combined with support from a 170 knot upper jet maximum to enhance the initial shot of snow in a narrow southwest to northeast band across the heart of West Virginia.  About a 40 to 50 mile wide corridor from near Huntington, on northeast through Winfield, Spencer, Glenville, Weston, Buckhannon, Elkins, and Philippi saw mostly 4 to 6 inches of snow accumulate in 6 hours or less.  Randolph County had 4 to 8 inches depending on elevation.  Just north and south of that band, snow amounts were less, mostly 1 to 3 inches.||The southern mountains including Fayette and Raleigh Counties had very little snow, starting mostly as sleet.  An increasing southeast wind caused surface temperatures to remain around 30 to 32, causing a period of freezing rain.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch were observed by midday. ||The precipitation decreased during the early afternoon as the support streaked toward the Mid Atlantic States.  Travel conditions improved during the afternoon.||Another batch of rain moved north during the evening of the 8th.  This renewed the freezing rain in portions of Fayette and Raleigh County.  The ice accretion reached a half inch around Cool Ridge in southern Raleigh County during the night. With slightly colder temperatures in the upper 20s to around 30 degrees, Pocahontas County received a quarter inch of ice during the evening, after receiving 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet during the day on the 8th.||In the higher terrain around Cool Ridge tree branches sagged, some snapped off. Power outages also occurred.  About 3,200 customers were reported without electricity in Raleigh County.

A strong autumn cold front swept across between 0100E and 0400E.  Strong winds existed just above the surface.  There were fast moving showers near the front, with just a few embedded thunderstorms.  The convection was able to mix some of the strong wind gusts down to the surface.||Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common in West Virginia.  Stronger wind gusts occurred in a few areas.    ||No injuries occurred.  Over 10,000 customers in Cabell County and over 8,000 customers in Wayne County were without electricity.  A few schools were closed for the day due to the power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.  The wind gust was estimated near 65 mph.

Rain showers began during the warm afternoon on the 5th, ahead of an approaching cold front.  In the wake of the frontal passage during that evening. the showers transformed into periods of rain.  The cold rain continued into the evening hours of the 6th as a low pressure wave rippled along the front.||Rains of 2 to 3 inches over 24 to 30 hours caused small streams and creeks to overflow in the usual vulnerable spots by the evening twilight on the 6th.  Minor river flooding even occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding was common throughout the county.  Roads were closed but structures were not affected. Straight Fork flooded and closed the road near the middle school near Griffithsville.  A water rescue was performed from a vehicle along Sugartree Fork.||An observer in Hamlin measured a total of 2.7 inches of rain by evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with record warmth in a large section of West Virginia.  Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s for much of the western lowlands. ||In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rain event spread over West Virginia during the early morning hours of Tuesday the 26th.  The rain was associated with a developing storm along the Gulf of Mexico coast.  The rain continued all day, as the storm lifted northeast.  Rain rates were mostly a tenth of an inch per hour.  The rain was a bit heavier during the late afternoon into the evening hours.  Rain totals of around 1.25 to 1.5 inches occurred over Webster County by late that evening. This resulted in swollen streams, but no flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After dark, around 2100E a man and a woman began to drive their ATV into the swollen waters of remote Sugar Creek.  The stream was still within its banks. Yet, they realized the water was too deep, and attempted to back out,  The vehicle overturned in the water.  The male made it out of the stream.  The female was carried downstream about a quarter of a mile.  Luckily, she found refuge on a fallen tree.||It took the male about an hour to reach a residence to call for help.  It took rescue workers about another hour to set up a high line rescue.   She was hoisted to safety in a harness.  She was taken to the local hospital and treated for hypothermia.

Temperatures fell into the single digits across the high terrain for the morning of the 17th.

A fast moving clipper system moved south of the Upper Ohio valley brushing areas from Pittsburgh south with snow. Across Tucker county much higher amounts of a very dry and fluffy snow fell. Amounts from the evening of January 31st into early morning of February 1sr were near 10 inches across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS COOP observer in Canaan Heights reported 10.7 inches of snow fell from the evening of January 31st into the early morning hours of February 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain showers began during the warm afternoon on the 5th, ahead of an approaching cold front.  In the wake of the frontal passage during that evening. the showers transformed into periods of rain.  The cold rain continued into the evening hours of the 6th as a low pressure wave rippled along the front.||Rains of 2 to 3 inches over 24 to 30 hours caused small streams and creeks to overflow in the usual vulnerable spots by the evening twilight on the 6th.  Minor river flooding even occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams overflowed onto roads during the evening of the 6th.  A motorist had to be rescued along Route 38 near Valley Furnace from waters of Brushy Fork.||Later, on the 7th into the 8th, the Tygart Valley River crested slightly more than a foot over the 14 foot flood stage around the Belington reach of the river.  This caused a few roads to be closed, such as the Talbot Road.

The Washington District Volunteer Fire Department pulled one person from a flooded vehicle.  Also, a girl had been washed into the stream.  Luckily, she held onto tree branches until she was rescued.  There were no injuries.||Later on the 7th, the Buckhannon River crested about a foot of the 23 foot flood stage in the community of Buckhannon.  This caused some minor flooding to roads in town, such as Madison, Marion, and Wood Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last of 3 distinct waves, along a stalled front to the east and south, passed during the day on the 17th.  It brought a quick shot of heavy snow, as far north as extreme southern West Virginia.  Three to 10 inches of wet snow fell in 6 hours in McDowell County.  The upper limit fell across the highest terrain toward Mercer County and the Virginia state line.   Lesser amounts, mostly 1 to 3 inches, fell further north in Wyoming County.

After a cold dawn, with temperatures on either side of zero, strong warm air advection developed light snow by midday on the 2nd.  One to 3 inches of snow was common into the evening hours.  After a late night lull, snow showers increased during the morning of the 3rd.  Mid level disturbances combined with plentiful low level moisture.  The snow showers decreased during the evening on the 4th.  In well over 24 hours, 4 to 8 inches of snow fell in the central and southern mountain counties with less in the lowlands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of less than a tenth of an inch was reported at Charles Town.

Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch were observed by midday. ||The precipitation decreased during the early afternoon as the support streaked toward the Mid Atlantic States.  Travel conditions improved during the afternoon.||Another batch of rain moved north during the evening of the 8th.  This renewed the freezing rain in portions of Fayette and Raleigh County.  The ice accretion reached a half inch around Cool Ridge in southern Raleigh County during the night. With slightly colder temperatures in the upper 20s to around 30 degrees, Pocahontas County received a quarter inch of ice during the evening, after receiving 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet during the day on the 8th.||In the higher terrain around Cool Ridge tree branches sagged, some snapped off. Power outages also occurred.  About 3,200 customers were reported without electricity in Raleigh County.

After a one day lull in the active weather on Saturday the 7th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain quickly spread back northeast into West Virginia by dawn on Sunday the 8th.||Strong low level warm air advection combined with support from a 170 knot upper jet maximum to enhance the initial shot of snow in a narrow southwest to northeast band across the heart of West Virginia.  About a 40 to 50 mile wide corridor from near Huntington, on northeast through Winfield, Spencer, Glenville, Weston, Buckhannon, Elkins, and Philippi saw mostly 4 to 6 inches of snow accumulate in 6 hours or less.  Randolph County had 4 to 8 inches depending on elevation.  Just north and south of that band, snow amounts were less, mostly 1 to 3 inches.||The southern mountains including Fayette and Raleigh Counties had very little snow, starting mostly as sleet.  An increasing southeast wind caused surface temperatures to remain around 30 to 32, causing a period of freezing rain.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch were observed by midday. ||The precipitation decreased during the early afternoon as the support streaked toward the Mid Atlantic States.  Travel conditions improved during the afternoon.||Another batch of rain moved north during the evening of the 8th.  This renewed the freezing rain in portions of Fayette and Raleigh County.  The ice accretion reached a half inch around Cool Ridge in southern Raleigh County during the night. With slightly colder temperatures in the upper 20s to around 30 degrees, Pocahontas County received a quarter inch of ice during the evening, after receiving 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet during the day on the 8th.||In the higher terrain around Cool Ridge tree branches sagged, some snapped off. Power outages also occurred.  About 3,200 customers were reported without electricity in Raleigh County.

The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with record warmth in a large section of West Virginia.  Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s for much of the western lowlands. ||In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a cold front, showers and thunderstorms survived during the late night or early morning hours.  Frequent lightning and brief downpours were common.  A few storms pulsed to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines near Route 85 and Robinson Creek.  A vehicle hit a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 30th, a convective line of showers formed along a pre-frontal surface trough and intensified as it moved east at 50 MPH. While only isolated thunderstorms occurred, the line produced convective wind damage across portions of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. The highest measured wind gusts were 67 MPH at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport in Latrobe, and 62 MPH at the Wheeling, WV airport. Heavy rainfall with this system caused some flooding of roadways over eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported numerous trees down in and around Morgantown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low pressure center brought a mix of winter precipitation to the upper Ohio Valley. Freezing rain accumulations of over one quarter inch occurred across portions of Tucker county West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. A light coating of freezing rain mixed with sleet fell over eastern Ohio and the remainder of northern West Virginia. Across western Pennsylvania light mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow produced up to one tenth of an inch of ice with 1 to 2 inches of snow.

A tight wind field behind an arctic cold front combined with temperatures in the single digits to near zero produced wind chills from 10 to 20 degrees below zero. The lowest readings were during the early morning hours of January 22nd and 23rd across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill readings across the county were below minus 10 late on the 21st into the morning of the 23rd. Surface reporting stations and COOP sites showed wind chills of minus 20 in the early mornings of the 22nd and 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front well in advance of low pressure across the Great Lakes produced a mix of winter precipitation across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland. Areas from Oil City to Dubois Pennsylvania received over one quarter inch of ice due to freezing rain, with amounts greater than one quarter inch also reported across Garrett county Maryland. Across the rest of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia up to one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated, with a light accumulation of sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of ice generally less than one tenth of an inch fell across portions of northern West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Albert Clipper type system moved across the Ohio Valley spreading light to moderate snow across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland during the 25th. Northwest winds behind the system produced snow showers into the day the 26th. Snowfall for the entire event along and south of a line from Lisbon, Ohio to Pittsburgh and Latrobe, Pennsylvania was 3 to 5 inches over the 2 days. During the 2 days across the higher elevations of northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland and the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges in Pennsylvania 4 to 7 inches fell. Areas from the northern suburbs of Pittsburgh to Interstate 80 in Pennsylvania received 1 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 3 to 5 inch snowfall occurred from the northern West Virginia Panhandle east to the higher elevations of Preston and parts of Tucker county. The highest amounts of  5 to 6 inches fell across parts of Preston county over the 2 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced scattered afternoon thunderstorms and small hail.
High pressure was wedged southward in the Mid Atlantic while low pressure deepened off the gulf coast. Below freezing surface temperatures and warm and moist air aloft produced freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of less than a tenth of an inch was reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system was over the Northern Plains.  High pressure to the north caused below freezing temperatures to advect southward into the Mid Atlantic.  Southerly flow aloft and ahead of low pressure led to above freezing temperatures above the below freezing surface.  Freezing rain occurred in most areas in the Mid Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice reports of around a trace were reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest upslope flow produced numerous snow squalls that reduced visibility to near zero at times and accumulated from 1 to 3 inches across the county. There was a significant number of accidents on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several accidents and delays were reported along U.S. Routes 52 and 460 due to the snow showers combined with winds gusting to near 30 mph. Snowfall amounts were roughly 1 to 3 inches over a 24-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary low pressure system formed over the Carolinas as the primary system moved northward and was located across the Central Plains/Ohio Valley.  High pressure over New England and northerly winds brought below freezing temperatures across the Potomac Highlands.  Over running precipitation was able to melt and re-freeze on contact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice amounts of around a trace were reported at surrounding locations.

Across Tucker county much higher amounts of a very dry and fluffy snow fell. Amounts from the evening of January 31st into early morning of February 1st were near 10 inches across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS COOP observer in Canaan Heights reported snowfall of 10.7 inches had fallen from  the evening of the 31st to the early morning of February 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the Mid Atlantic in the morning and showers and thunderstorms moved across the highlands. Strong winds above the surface were able to mix down and damaging wind gusts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were power lines down that started a brush fire on Grassy Lick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Potomac Highlands late in the evening. Showers and thunderstorms were able to tap into a strong low level jet and bring strong gusts to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down blocking part of the city route 465.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Potomac Highlands late in the evening. Showers and thunderstorms were able to tap into a strong low level jet and bring strong gusts to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree blocking the southbound lane of Route 220 near the Hickory Hill Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in the evening along a weak outflow boundary. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across the West Virginia panhandle and Washington county, Pennsylvania.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in the evening along a weak outflow boundary. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across the West Virginia panhandle and Washington county, Pennsylvania.
A strong cold front moved across the Potomac Highlands late in the evening. Showers and thunderstorms were able to tap into a strong low level jet and bring strong gusts to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous tree limbs down near the Mountain Institute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front cross the mountains in the evening and below freezing temperatures and westerly flow produced snow showers along the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around three inches was reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and complex storm system moved across the Ohio Valley from March 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. Snowfall in Garrett county Maryland, Tucker and Preston counties in west Virginia, and the ridges of Fayette and Westmoreland counties received anywhere from 6 to 12 inches of snow in 12 hours. A heavier band of snow in Pennsylvania brought accumulations from 8 to 12 inches across portions of Allegheny, Washington, and Westmoreland counties, with far southern portions of Butler, Armstrong, and Indiana counties receiving 6 to 12 inches. Elsewhere across across eastern Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia 2 to 6 inches of snow fell.

High pressure over New England and northerly winds brought below freezing temperatures across the Potomac Highlands.  Over running precipitation was able to melt and re-freeze on contact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice amounts of around a trace were estimated above 1500 feet and reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Tennessee Valley pushed a warm front across the upper Ohio Valley during the early morning into the late afternoon of the 18th. A general light mix of freezing rain, snow and sleet fell across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Due to the quick movement of the front, snowfall amounts averaged from 1 to 4 inches, with average freezing rain and sleet accumulations between one tenth and two tenths of an inch. The exceptions to this were across Preston county in West Virginia and Lawrence county in Pennsylvania, where precipitation stayed freezing rain a bit longer for over one quarter inch of ice to accumulate.
High pressure was wedged southward in the Mid Atlantic while low pressure deepened off the gulf coast. Below freezing surface temperatures and warm and moist air aloft produced freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of less than a tenth of an inch was reported by surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure was centered off the New England coast.  A disturbance with enough moisture produced snow showers with accumulating snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around 3 inches were reported at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure was centered off the New England coast.  A disturbance with enough moisture produced snow showers with accumulating snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around 5 inches were reported at Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure off the New England coast caused a cold northwest flow over the mountains of West Virginia. The northwest wind picked up moisture from the Great Lakes, causing snow showers across locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled around six inches in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system moved east across the upper Ohio Valley the evening of the 24th before the storm transitioned to a large coastal low later in the day of the 25th. Snowfall amounts from 8 to 12 inches in 12 hours fell in the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania. A band of heavier snow also fell over much of Greene county in southwest Pennsylvania where an average of 6 inches of snow fell in under 12 hours. Elsewhere across northern West Virginia 2 to 5 inches of snow fell.

The Mid Atlantic was located between low pressure to the west and east. This led to northerly flow overnight. Low pressure to the east intensified as it moved northward. Surface temperatures were marginal and the rain-snow line was close to the I-95 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around three inches were reported at Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved through the region in northwest flow aloft.  This clipper system produced short lived snow bands that produced several inches of snow in the higher terrain including the Allegheny Front and Potomac Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around 6 inches were reported in Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved over the Mid Atlantic as low pressure continued to intensify east of the region.  Cold air advection and westerly flow led to enhancements of snowfall in the up slope regions of the Allegheny Front including Pendleton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of three inches were reported in Cherry Grove.

A warm front lifted north, through West Virginia, during the day on Wednesday.  Heating and instability increased greatly during the afternoon of the 12th.  Dew points reached into the lower 70s across the lowlands.  Disturbances in the mid and upper levels rippled southeast from the lower Great Lakes initially.  Additional disturbances then moved east overnight into Thursday the 13th.   ||The first round of showers and thunderstorms formed across in northern Ohio during the early evening of the 12th, then raced southeast into northern West Virginia as the new calendar day began.||Those initial cells did become repetitive in a few spots, such as northern Taylor County north of Grafton. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved overhead while cool dry air filtered into the region.  Clear skies led to radiational cooling overnight for the western slopes of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A temperature of 30 degrees was reported at Mill Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west interacted with a very warm and moist tropical air mass.||A corridor of showers and thunderstorms persisted throughout the day Wednesday from Southeastern Kentucky and Southwestern Virginia, northeastward through southeastern West Virginia.  This area intensified and expanded in the afternoon heat. Other thunderstorms developed in the afternoon heat around the lower Ohio River. The complex weakened while moving further east in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were down at the corner of Coal Run Road and County Route 50/27.

A mid and upper level low developed over West Virginia on the 12th, then drifted into southeast Ohio by that evening.  This stopped the expected dry air intrusion along the Ohio Valley.  The low level flow brought an increase in moisture again from the east.||Rain showers developed over the mountainous counties by midday.  Plenty of sunshine in the western lowlands increased instability.  Clusters of convection developed during the afternoon with a slow west or southwest drift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1.25 to 1.75 inches in an hour or less over wet terrain caused small streams to flood roads.  Streams such as Sugar Creek, Kanawha Two Mile, Legg Fork, and Magazine Branch flooded.||A couple of parked vehicles were damaged by flood waters from Kanawha Two Mile.  The water from Kanawha Two Mile also got under one mobile home and damaged some insulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability, moisture, and afternoon heating helped develop showers and thunderstorms in West Virginia during the mid and late afternoon.  Two separate clusters of storms consolidated over portions of Wyoming and McDowell Counties during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1 to 2 inches in about 2 hours were common.  Near Litwar, a 1.4 inch measurement was obtained.  However, weather radar estimated 2.5 to 4 inches of rain over a substantial area around Garland and Beartown. ||Minor flash flooding occurred along small streams such as Betsy Branch.  Even the headwaters of the Clear Fork overflowed onto roads around Coalwood.  The headwaters of the Dry Fork were out of its banks near Berwind.  No damage to structures occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A local downpour formed along a weakening cold front during the evening in the Charleston vicinity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One flood prone low spot along Valley Hill Drive near Cross Lanes saw water get into 2 houses after a brief downpour over wet terrain.  The location is along a tributary to Rocky Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heating combined with weak disturbance in the southerly winds aloft to trigger a few thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees fell on Lamont Drive, causing some damage to 4 dwellings and electric lines. Describing the wet ground, a resident noted the big mud hole left in the ground after one tree fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eastern fringe of a deep moist southern flow, north to south bands of thunderstorms during the late afternoon of southern West Virginia.  They formed on a west to east thermal gradient. Temperatures reached the mid 80s around Charleston, while 70s prevailed under more layered clouds further to the west. ||Thunderstorms pulsed to severe limits in the eastern section of Charleston, on north toward Pinch and Elkview.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches fell in the Kanawha City section of the city.  A few streets were blocked.  One large fallen tree damaged a house on Washington Ave.  The tree crashed down into the family room and a bedroom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north, through West Virginia, during the day on Wednesday.  Heating and instability increased greatly during the afternoon of the 12th.  Dew points reached into the lower 70s across the lowlands.  Disturbances in the mid and upper levels rippled southeast from the lower Great Lakes initially.  Additional disturbances then moved east overnight into Thursday the 13th.   ||The first round of showers and thunderstorms formed across in northern Ohio during the early evening of the 12th, then raced southeast into northern West Virginia as the new calendar day began.||Those initial cells did become repetitive in a few spots, such as northern Taylor County north of Grafton.  This did not cause any serious flooding, but helped saturate the ground.||Meanwhile, another larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved out of northern Indiana, through Ohio, reaching into West Virginia during the predawn hours.  The southern edge of this complex became oriented west to east, along the fast flow aloft.  As a result, repetitive cells did dump heavy rain from Jackson County through southern Roane, southern Calhoun, southern Gilmer, Braxton, and Webster Counties.  Rain amounts over 2 inches were common in that west to east band.  The most serious of the flooding occurred in Roane County.   By late July, this led to a Presidential disaster declaration.  See FEMA DR-4132 for more details. ||A break in the rain occurred during the mid to late morning hours on the 13th.  A few smaller showers and thunderstorms did form over north central West Virginia during this time frame.  The final round of strong to severe showers and thunderstorms streaked east at 50 mph, out of Ohio, reaching West Virginia after 1000E on the 13th.   This final round was also associated with the surface cold front.  Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph were common.  Yet, the leading edge did become stronger as it headed into southeastern counties of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst was associated with a developing bow echo configuration of showers and thunderstorms.  A narrow path of damage started just down the Little Kanawha River from Elizabeth and ended near a cemetery.   The maximum width of damage was about 400 yards. ||Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped.  Two houses were damaged by fallen trees.  A porch has its roof flipped onto the attached house.   A metal shed, not anchored to the ground, was destroyed.  The shed was blown about 70 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region in the form of a squall line of storms. Winds of 60 to 70 mph accompanied this squall line, resulting a widespread wind damage. Large hail also accompanied some of the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm squall line blew down dozens trees in several communities including Matoka, Kegley, and Princeton. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South of a cold front, mid level support and upper level jet dynamics helped form and enhance showers and thunderstorms in eastern Ohio during the late evening on the 12th.  These clusters trained east into north central West Virginia near the Route 50 corridor. In this maximum rainfall band, amounts were on the order of 2 to 4 inches from about 0000E to 0400E on the 13th.  Similar to Ohio, the greatest observed rainfall in West Virginia also occurred along the Ohio River.  A spotter in Williamstown measured 4.41 inches of rain, while further down the river a spotter in Vienna had 3.05 inches. In Pleasants County, an automatic gauge in Saint Marys measured 3.9 inches.  In southern Tyler County, another automatic gauge measured 3.5 inches at Conway Lake.  The gauges near Ellenboro and Cairo in Ritchie County measured between 1.5 to 2 inches, but amounts were likely heavier just to their north.  Heading further east in West Virginia, rain amounts gradually lowered.  The cooperative observer in West Union had 2.9 inches.  An automatic gauge in northern Harrison County along Little Ten Mile Creek near Brown had 2.59 inches.  Maximum rainfall in Taylor County was also around 2.5 inches.  A gauge along Laurel Run had 2.55 inches.||Small stream flooding was widespread in this west to east rainfall band.  In the most vulnerable spots, houses or mobile homes were damaged.  Several parked vehicles were flooded.  Damage occurred to roads and culverts.  Small private bridges were damaged.  A strong rise occurred on the Middle Island Creek in Tyler and Pleasants County.  The stream gauge near Little saw the water level increase 13 feet in the vertical.||Thankfully, no injuries or deaths occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams flooded, closed, and damaged roads.  One example, was County Route 7/5 or Mahaley Hollow Road.  The approach to a bridge over Little Sancho Creek was washed out.  The creek had crested over the top of the bridge.  The road along Jefferson Run had its foundation undermined in places.  A house was flooded off of Route 18 along Indian Creek.  It was the first day of the new school year for the county.  Yet, classes were canceled since many bus routes were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ohio county emergency manager reported that North Fork Short Creek is out of its banks and has portions of North Fork Short Creek Road submerged. The road has been closed and a shelter opened for affected residents. The emergency manager also reported that Short Creek is at bank-full.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system passed across Virginia during the afternoon and evening. With light upper level winds, widespread showers and thunderstorms were slow moving, and resulted in flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on several low water crossings across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system passed across Virginia during the afternoon and evening. With light upper level winds, widespread showers and thunderstorms were slow moving, and resulted in flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Interstate 64 exit ramp at mile marker 175 is closed due to water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong summer disturbance in the winds aloft moved up the Ohio Valley during the morning.  It combined with the muggy air to produce local downpours during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1 to 2 inches fell in about an hour.  The usual street flooding and ponding of water in poor drainage areas in downtown Huntington occurred.  The viaducts were flooded.  An ambulance bay at a city hospital collected water from poor drainage.   About the city, some vehicles stalled in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across the Mid Atlantic and low pressure formed and moved along it. Southerly flow advected gulf moisture into the region and showers and thunderstorms easily formed. Heavy rain, hail, damaging winds and tornadoes occurred throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several trees down around Moorefield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downpour occurred between 0500 and 0700E, followed by a lull, with a final downpour around 1045E to 1115E. ||The heaviest band was oriented west to east along the Route 33 corridor on south to the headwaters of the Pocatalico River.  The heaviest rain was on the order of 1.5 to near 2 inches in an hour shortly after dawn.   Near Looneyville, the heaviest measured rate was 1.8 inches between 0545 and 0645E.  Spencer had 1.4 inches between 0500 and 0600E with near 2 inches by 0700E.  All total, the measured amounts including the final midday downpour was 2.8 inches at the National Weather Service cooperative observer in Spencer and the automatic gauge near Spencer.  The gauge at Looneyville had 2.5 inches.  However, total storm amounts over 3 inches were possible between Spencer and Looneyville. ||The Spring Creek basin, including Tanner Fork, flooded the county seat of Spencer.  The usual low spots like along Front Street and Church Street were flooded. The low income apartment complex for senior citizens flooded.  Water was over 1 foot deep on the first floor of several of those apartments.  The Route 119 corridor near Walmart flooded.  The creek did not cross over the top of Route 119, but backwater from feeder runs and urban drainage collected in the residential area on the west side. This included the Reynolds and Bell Streets vicinity.  Homes in the Alvord section of town, that usually do not see water, had water.  Blocked culverts in front of the 911 Center contributed to the evacuation of the 911 Center including the office of emergency services.    Water also flooded the ambulance authority building.  The local feed and garden store was flooded.  Water surrounded a pharmacy store.  Several water rescues were performed.||Further south, the lowlands around Walton were flooded by the Pocatalico River, including along River Road.  Water got into the Walton Post Office.  The local middle school had runoff enter the building from the adjacent hillsides.   ||About 40 people were displaced by the flooding.  A Red Cross shelter was set up in the local armory.  Around 7 people stayed there overnight.||Governor Tomblin declared a state of emergency for Roane County.  National Guard troops were placed on active duty to help do wellness checks and ship supplies.||State individual damage assessment counted 1 manufactured home destroyed.  In terms of single family homes, none were destroyed, 47 had major damage, with another 41 with minor damage.  One multifamily dwelling had major damage, with 14 others sustaining minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic led to southerly flow to transport warm humid conditions in the Mid Atlantic.  A trough axis led to the development of thunderstorms across the west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down at the intersection of Rt 50 and 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level disturbance in the winds aloft caused showers and thunderstorms to form and expand over southern West Virginia as the new month began.  The convection moved north and northwest.  Movement was only around 15 to 20 mph and repetitive cells affected small portions of Wayne County.||Another round of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through the western counties of West Virginia during that evening.  An arch of convection came out of eastern Kentucky and joined forces with another area of showers moving north off the western slopes of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 2 to 2.5 inches fell in 3 hours or less.  A gauge near East Lynn Lake measured 2.3 inches between 0000E and 0300E.  Several culverts and private driveway bridges were damaged or washed out.  Wilson Creek and Millers Fork were 2 of the small creeks that caused damage.

An arch of convection came out of eastern Kentucky and joined forces with another area of showers moving north off the western slopes of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in just over an hour over wet terrain.  One observer measured 1.4 inches in 50 minutes along Angel Fork.  ||Angel Fork flooded Route 1/3.  Browns Creek also flooded.  Serious street flooding occurred in Dunbar and Nitro.  A water rescue occurred in Cross Lanes.       ||No dwellings were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic led to southerly flow to transport warm humid conditions in the Mid Atlantic.  A trough axis led to the development of thunderstorms across the west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A willow tree was uprooted and a large tree broke in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic led to southerly flow to transport warm humid conditions in the Mid Atlantic.  A trough axis led to the development of thunderstorms across the west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down at the intersection of South Mineral Street and Fort Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic led to southerly flow to transport warm humid conditions in the Mid Atlantic.  A trough axis led to the development of thunderstorms across the west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a large tree limb down at East 5th and South Reymann on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid environment existed over West Virginia with surface dew points in the lower 70s.  A weak steering flow existed.  Several small clusters of slow moving showers and embedded thunderstorms formed over West Virginia on the 8th, well south of a cold front across northern Ohio.||The front sagged slowly south into the 10th.  Another round of convection occurred near dawn on the 10th in the Huntington to Charleston corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains estimated at 2 to 2.5 inches in less than 3 hours fell between 1200E and 1500E over the headwaters of Grasslick Creek in southern Jackson County.  This was mainly north of Bibbe Ridge and west of Shinn Ridge.   Grasslick Creek and its small feeder runs, such as Pleasant Valley Run, flooded roads and surrounded a few houses and garages.  At the mouth of Pleasant Valley Run, near the Southern Jackson County Fire Department, water was about a foot over Route 21.  One water rescue took place from a vehicle on Route 21.  Water got into the Kenna Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west interacted with a very warm and moist tropical air mass.||A corridor of showers and thunderstorms persisted throughout the day Wednesday from Southeastern Kentucky and Southwestern Virginia, northeastward through southeastern West Virginia.  This area intensified and expanded in the afternoon heat. Other thunderstorms developed in the afternoon heat around the lower Ohio River.  A massive complex of thunderstorms expanded and intensified as it moved up the Ohio Valley and approached from the west.  ||Marginally severe wind accompanied the line of thunderstorms through southeast Ohio and across the Ohio River.  The complex weakened while moving further east in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1 to 2 inches likely fell in less than an hour.  Small streams flooded roads.   ||About 8 to 10 inches of water crossed roadways in Lower Vienna, West Virginia. The water over roads generally covers an area between 18th and 40th Streets including 12th to 30th streets. Flooding originated from Pond Run, a small creek that flows into the Ohio River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west interacted with a very warm and moist tropical air mass.||A corridor of showers and thunderstorms persisted throughout the day Wednesday from Southeastern Kentucky and Southwestern Virginia, northeastward through southeastern West Virginia.  This area intensified and expanded in the afternoon heat. Other thunderstorms developed in the afternoon heat around the lower Ohio River.  A massive complex of thunderstorms expanded and intensified as it moved up the Ohio Valley and approached from the west.  ||Marginally severe wind accompanied the line of thunderstorms through southeast Ohio and across the Ohio River.  The complex weakened while moving further east in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred for a vehicle at the intersection of Legg Fork Road and Spring Branch Road in Sissonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 17th.  This was well east of the much larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms over Kentucky.||In the humid air and with mid level support, that larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms surrvived overnight and crossed through West Virginia during the morning and early afternoon of the 18th.  Street flooding was reported in various spots.  Due to the wetter ground, minor flash flooding occurred in a portion of Roane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small but nearly stationary thunderstorm formed near Beaver during the evening of the 17th, then slowly built back toward the northwest and into the city of Beckley.  Rainfall was estimated at over 2 inches in less than 2 hours.  The lingering shower rained itself out and dissipated after 2200E.||Urban street flooding and poor drainage flooding was common.  Yet, at the peak the flooding became more serious.  Occupants of one vehicle were pulled from a vehicle when water was almost over the top of the car.  Whitestick Creek flooded near Mabscott.  Water reached the decks of homes around Nebraska and Prince Streets in Beckley.  One private retaining wall in Beckley was washed out, with debris coming out onto the road.  Basement flooding was common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north, through West Virginia, during the day on Wednesday.  Heating and instability increased greatly during the afternoon of the 12th.  Dew points reached into the lower 70s across the lowlands.  Disturbances in the mid and upper levels rippled southeast from the lower Great Lakes initially.  Additional disturbances then moved east overnight into Thursday the 13th.   ||The first round of showers and thunderstorms formed across in northern Ohio during the early evening of the 12th, then raced southeast into northern West Virginia as the new calendar day began.||Those initial cells did become repetitive in a few spots, such as northern Taylor County north of Grafton.  This did not cause any serious flooding, but helped saturate the ground.||Meanwhile, another larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved out of northern Indiana, through Ohio, reaching into West Virginia during the predawn hours.  The southern edge of this complex became oriented west to east, along the fast flow aloft.  As a result, repetitive cells did dump heavy rain from Jackson County through southern Roane, southern Calhoun, southern Gilmer, Braxton, and Webster Counties.  Rain amounts over 2 inches were common in that west to east band.  The most serious of the flooding occurred in Roane County.   By late July, this led to a Presidential disaster declaration.  See FEMA DR-4132 for more details. ||A break in the rain occurred during the mid to late morning hours on the 13th.  A few smaller showers and thunderstorms did form over north central West Virginia during this time frame.  The final round of strong to severe showers and thunderstorms streaked east at 50 mph, out of Ohio, reaching West Virginia after 1000E on the 13th.   This final round was also associated with the surface cold front.  Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph were common.  Yet, the leading edge did become stronger as it headed into southeastern counties of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Route 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the humidity of a waning heat wave, clusters of showers and thunderstorms starting forming by midday in West Virginia on the 19th.||Their outflow boundaries, along with differential heating and a mid level disturbance, helped develop other areas of showers and thunderstorm into the evening.||Downpours and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 2 to 3 inches fell in less than 3 hoursl.  Hambrick Ridge measured 2.14 inches.  ||Creeks, such as Five Mile Creek and Crab Creek, flooded roads and damaged a few houses.  Along Crab Creek, 1 small home was lifted off its cinder block foundation and floated several hundred yards until coming to rest against a tree.  The woman inside escaped just before the house moved.   Vehicles stalled in high water along Crab Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow, with disturbances aloft, dominated the area for this episode. A west to east frontal system lay well to the north, across northern Ohio early Wednesday morning.||One large complex of storms dropped southeast through portions of north central West Virginia into the northern mountains of West Virginia during the predawn hours.   Maximum rain amounts of 2 to 3 inches fell 3 to 4 hours over portions of Harrison, Taylor, and Barbour Counties.  This caused some minor flash flooding.   ||The cold front moved further south during the day on the 28th, to near a Pittsburgh to Columbus line by late afternoon. After a lull in the rain, the afternoon heating combined with the instability and moisture to cause another round of thunderstorms and showers during the late afternoon and early evening. ||The heaviest rains with this second round were slightly further south in West Virginia.  The maximum amounts were also less than the predawn round, mostly in the 1 to 2 inch range.  Yet, due to the wet ground, some minor flash flooding occurred again.  One storm pulsed to a severe level in Braxton County.||The maximum rain totals from the 2 rounds were 3 to 5 inches.   ||In the wake of these rains, strong rises occurred along the Tygart Valley, the Greenbrier, and the Cheat River basins.  On the 29th, the Tygart Valley River at Belington created just above its 14 foot flood stage, but caused no significant damage.  Along the Shavers Fork, some camp grounds near Bowden were evacuated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second crest in streams occurred early in the evening.  Another round of minor flash flooding resulted. Water overflowed out of streams and onto roads.  Route 76 was again covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the more humid environment, ahead of a mid and upper level disturbance, areas of thunderstorms formed by late morning in southern West Virginia.  Surface dew points had risen into the lower 70s. ||After pulsing briefly to local downpour levels, the storms would weaken.  Meanwhile, new cells would form on their perimeters in the adjacent unmodified environment.||The storms lingered into the evening and stayed the strongest near the mid level trough axis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One batch of showers and thunderstorms moved through the Rocky Fork vicinity of western Kanawha County during the midday. This included along Route 622, including Martin Branch and Fisher Branch.  An estimated 1.5 to 2 inches of rain in about 90 minutes from 1100E to 1230E. Ditch lines overflowed and poor drainage areas became large pools of standing water.  Clogged culverts also caused water to flow onto roads. Minor road damage occurred including Berry Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level ridge was firmly anchored across the southern/central Appalachians with a very warm and humid air mass setting the stage for thunderstorm development. One storm dropped hail over Monroe County and another produced some flash flooding  along the Summers/Greenbrier county line. Radar estimated 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in this area.

A large upper level ridge was firmly anchored across the southern/central Appalachians with a very warm and humid air mass setting the stage for thunderstorm development. One storm dropped hail over Monroe County and another produced some flash flooding  along the Summers/Greenbrier county line. Radar estimated 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road was washed out halfway between Lawn and Dawson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duncan Branch Road flooded from water overflowing adjacent drainage ditches and culverts. The Lawn-Dawson Road was also washed out near the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South of a cold front, mid level support and upper level jet dynamics helped form and enhance showers and thunderstorms in eastern Ohio during the late evening on the 12th.  These clusters trained east into north central West Virginia near the Route 50 corridor. In this maximum rainfall band, amounts were on the order of 2 to 4 inches from about 0000E to 0400E on the 13th.  Similar to Ohio, the greatest observed rainfall in West Virginia also occurred along the Ohio River.  A spotter in Williamstown measured 4.41 inches of rain, while further down the river a spotter in Vienna had 3.05 inches. In Pleasants County, an automatic gauge in Saint Marys measured 3.9 inches.  In southern Tyler County, another automatic gauge measured 3.5 inches at Conway Lake.  The gauges near Ellenboro and Cairo in Ritchie County measured between 1.5 to 2 inches, but amounts were likely heavier just to their north.  Heading further east in West Virginia, rain amounts gradually lowered.  The cooperative observer in West Union had 2.9 inches.  An automatic gauge in northern Harrison County along Little Ten Mile Creek near Brown had 2.59 inches.  Maximum rainfall in Taylor County was also around 2.5 inches.  A gauge along Laurel Run had 2.55 inches.||Small stream flooding was widespread in this west to east rainfall band.  In the most vulnerable spots, houses or mobile homes were damaged.  Several parked vehicles were flooded.  Damage occurred to roads and culverts.  Small private bridges were damaged.  A strong rise occurred on the Middle Island Creek in Tyler and Pleasants County.  The stream gauge near Little saw the water level increase 13 feet in the vertical.||Thankfully, no injuries or deaths occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams flooded across the northern portion of the county.  The most serious flooding appeared to be along Little Ten Mile Creek and its feeder runs.  Water damaged Route 20.  Off of Route 20 along Trouser Leg, water crested about a foot on the outside walls of a church. Water surrounded a few houses and yards, and rose around parked vehicles.  Cunningham Run along Route 8 was another one of the streams that flooded.

Meanwhile, new cells would form on their perimeters in the adjacent unmodified environment.||The storms lingered into the evening and stayed the strongest near the mid level trough axis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down along Kanawha Turnpike, knocking down some power lines.  One tree was 3 feet in diameter.

On the 29th, the Tygart Valley River at Belington created just above its 14 foot flood stage, but caused no significant damage.  Along the Shavers Fork, some camp grounds near Bowden were evacuated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second round of showers and storms dumped 1 to 1.5 inches of rain on wet ground.  Small streams overflowed onto roads.   Some examples included along Kings Run and Chenoweth Creek.  Water collected in low spots and surrounded some mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South of a cold front, mid level support and upper level jet dynamics helped form and enhance showers and thunderstorms in eastern Ohio during the late evening on the 12th.  These clusters trained east into north central West Virginia near the Route 50 corridor. In this maximum rainfall band, amounts were on the order of 2 to 4 inches from about 0000E to 0400E on the 13th.  Similar to Ohio, the greatest observed rainfall in West Virginia also occurred along the Ohio River.  A spotter in Williamstown measured 4.41 inches of rain, while further down the river a spotter in Vienna had 3.05 inches. In Pleasants County, an automatic gauge in Saint Marys measured 3.9 inches.  In southern Tyler County, another automatic gauge measured 3.5 inches at Conway Lake.  The gauges near Ellenboro and Cairo in Ritchie County measured between 1.5 to 2 inches, but amounts were likely heavier just to their north.  Heading further east in West Virginia, rain amounts gradually lowered.  The cooperative observer in West Union had 2.9 inches.  An automatic gauge in northern Harrison County along Little Ten Mile Creek near Brown had 2.59 inches.  Maximum rainfall in Taylor County was also around 2.5 inches.  A gauge along Laurel Run had 2.55 inches.||Small stream flooding was widespread in this west to east rainfall band.  In the most vulnerable spots, houses or mobile homes were damaged.  Several parked vehicles were flooded.  Damage occurred to roads and culverts.  Small private bridges were damaged.  A strong rise occurred on the Middle Island Creek in Tyler and Pleasants County.  The stream gauge near Little saw the water level increase 13 feet in the vertical.||Thankfully, no injuries or deaths occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred when streams across the northern and eastern portion of the county overflowed. Little Sandy Creek flooded Eby Road near its intersection with Route 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of convection formed in east central Ohio around 0000E Friday.  It rolled slowly southeastward through Morgan and Washington Counties in Ohio and then on into northern West Virginia before turning east.  A flash flood warning was issued early Friday morning for Doddridge, Ritchie, Pleasants, Tyler and eastern Wood Counties in West Virginia.  In West Virginia, the minor flooding occurred in Tyler County where rain amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indian Creek flowed out of its banks and over Indian Creek Road, resulting in the road being closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the more humid environment, ahead of a mid and upper level disturbance, areas of thunderstorms formed by late morning in southern West Virginia.  Surface dew points had risen into the lower 70s. ||After pulsing briefly to local downpour levels, the storms would weaken. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 61 mph was measured by the FAA tower wind equipment on the field, that was being used as the back-up to the ASOS equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtropical ridge was over the Mid Atlantic with northwesterly flow leading to hot and humid conditions.  This led to a unstable atmosphere and thunderstorms formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a large tree down into a house on Wall Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall across western Pennsylvania and the northern West Virginia Panhandle. Flooding of roadways and areas of poor drainage were reported.  Significant flash flooding was reported across portions of Fayette county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Big Grave Creek out of its banks and flooding residences.

This caused some minor flash flooding.   ||The cold front moved further south during the day on the 28th, to near a Pittsburgh to Columbus line by late afternoon. The maximum amounts were also less than the predawn round, mostly in the 1 to 2 inch range.  Yet, due to the wet ground, some minor flash flooding occurred again. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of showers and thunderstorms crossed Kanawha County on the 21st in the evening just after sunset.  This was about 8 hours after the first round of showers.  Rains of  .9 to nearly 2 inches fell in an hour.  The heaviest measured amount was 1.9 inches from an automatic gauge near Big Chimney.  A National Weather Service employee in the East End of Charleston measured 0.90 in an hour. ||Street flooding was common from Saint Albans and Cross Lanes into the Charleston vicinity.  Underpasses were flooded.  Water flowed off some of steeper hillsides onto roads and into yards.  This included along the Kanawha Turnpike in South Charleston. Smith Creek Road was damaged.  A few vehicles stalled in the deepest water resulting in some water rescues.  Water surrounded a house along Doc Bailey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North of a strong east to west front, a narrow band of snow streaked into the central West Virginia mountains around 0800E on the 17th.  Two to 5 inches of snow fell over southern Upshur County, Randolph County and the northern half of Pocahontas County by 1800E.  For example, Bartow in northern Pocahontas County reported 4 to 5 inches of snow accumulation.  At the extreme highest elevations, the maximum snow accumulation was around 6 inches, including the Snowshoe vicinity.  In the western lowlands, an inch or 2 of snow managed to accumulate in a few counties, including Mason, Jackson, and Roane.||An increasing flow from the east and southeast, causing lingering sleet, freezing rain, and freezing drizzle over eastern Randolph and Pocahontas County through the morning of the 18th.  Only a thin film of ice occurred.

A storm system moved northeast out of south central Kentucky during the afternoon of the 24th.  The low pressure system reached northeast Ohio on the morning of the 25th.  Snow began during midday on the 24th in Pocahontas County on a cooling upslope wind from the southeast.  The snow spread north into the northern lowlands during the afternoon.  A lull occurred during the late afternoon and early evening.   An additional shot of snow was mixed with rain during the evening before changing over to frequent snow showers during the morning of the 25th.  ||Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were common by dawn on the 25th, north of the Route 50 corridor in the northern lowlands, including the Clarksburg vicinity.  Despite starting mostly as rain, a 2 to 3 inch accumulation of snow was also observed over the Barbour, Upshur, Nicholas, Fayette, and Raleigh by dawn on the 25th.   Five to 8 inches were common by dawn on the 25th in Pocahontas, Webster, and Randolph Counties.  Snow showers continued to accumulate in the mountain counties into the afternoon on the 26th with another 3 to 8 inches of accumulation. The snow depth at Snowshoe went from 4 inches prior to this episode to 15 inches on the 26th. By dawn on the 27th, Snowshoe snow depth was 20 inches.  In the lower terrain of these mountainous counties, a snow depth of 4 to 8 inches was common at dawn on the 26th. ||Meanwhile, after having rain on the 24th, Wyoming and McDowell Counties finally saw 1 to 4 inches of snow accumulation from the 25th into the 26th.

With low pressure in the lower Ohio Valley and high pressure off the New England coast, a strong southeast wind flow developed during the day on the 26th.  Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph were common over the high terrain in the mountainous counties.  The strongest measured gusts occurred over the favored terrain across the southern mountains. The Raleigh County airport near Beckley measured a gust to 53 mph near 1400E.||The same southeast winds kept temperatures in the 25 to 30 degree range into the early afternoon across Pocahontas, plus eastern and southern Randolph County.  A quarter to a half inch of ice accumulated on trees and elevated metal surfaces from freezing rain during the mid morning into the early afternoon.  The mountain ridges, such as Cheat Mountain, had the ice amounts near a half inch.  Due to the daylight hour and time of year, most roads were just wet.  A few tree branches did fall, from the combination of the ice accretion and the wind.   The melted down liquid amounts were mostly in the .3 to .4 range.

A late season snow brought an accumulation of 1 to 2 inches to Pocahontas County. ||An easterly flow into initially dry air, helped cool the boundary layer and form the snow during the late afternoon or early evening of the 4th.  The snow diminished and ended as some drizzle or freezing drizzle early on the 5th.

In response to a strong mid level circulation, a low pressure strengthened as it moved up the Ohio River Valley during the daylight hours on the 5th.  It spread some light rain into southern West Virginia.   With an easterly flow into the mountains, boundary layer temperatures were colder in the central mountains and northern lowlands.||As the low center moves to near Point Pleasant on the evening of the 5th, snow developed over the central mountains.  A secondary low pressure developed in North Carolina,  The 2 systems consolidated over the Virginia coast by dawn on the 6th.||The snow diminished by midday on the 6th, with flurries and drizzle lingering into the night.   Accumulations of 5 to 12 inches were common in about 18 hours throughout Pocahontas and Randolph Counties.   At Snowshoe, the snow depth increased from 12 inches prior to the storm, to 22 inches.  The combination of an old snow cover and this storm also left Kumbrabow State Forest with a total snow depth of 22 inches. ||Webster County had lesser amounts, ranging from 2 inches in Webster Springs to 8 inches on Point Mountain.   Even lower amounts, mostly 1 to 3 inches, fell near Clarksburg, Grafton, Philippi, and Buckhannnon.    ||The heavy wet snow caused tree branches to fall onto power lines in Pocahontas County.  Roughly 3,100 customers in Pocahontas County lost electricity on the 6th.  Over 24 hours after the storm had ended, roughly half of those affected were still without electricity.  A warming shelter was set up in Green Bank by the county.

An arctic cold front swept through during the early morning hours on the 1st.  The on and off snow showers, that fell during Jan 31st, became a steadier heavier snow by 0000E.  The snow diminished to snow showers by 1000E.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common in Upshur, Randolph, Webster, Nicholas, and Pocahontas Counties.  Amounts of 2 to 3 inches were widespread in the southern coal fields and western lowlands.

A classic eastern U.S. cold air wedge set up began to evolve from December 7th into December 8th as a large 1038mb Canadian surface high, anchored across eastern Pennsylvania and eastern New York wedged southwestward along the east side of the Appalachians down through Virginia into Georgia.  Meanwhile, a cold front was stalled along the southeast U.S. coast.   In the upper-levels of the atmosphere a deep western U.S. trough was slowly shifting eastward into the central U.S.  Surface low pressure was tracking along the stalled southeast U.S. frontal boundary.  This feature combined with the slowly eastward tracking upper-level trough provided for the development of widespread overrunning precipitation into the cold air mass where temperatures hovered in the upper 20s and lower 30s.  Given that a layer of warmer air with temperatures above freezing was present just 3000 to 4000 feet above the surface, precipitation fell largely in the cold air at the surface as a wintry mix of freezing rain and sleet, with some snow in the northern portions and higher elevations of the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA).  This was a near perfect cold air damming/wedge pattern for the mid-Atlantic region and as such proved to be so in terms of winter weather.  Freezing rain with ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 to 1/2 inch was widespread across southeastern West Virginia.  Significant ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were reported from many areas of the four counties in southeast West Virginia within the Blacksburg CWA.  Warm temperatures in the days leading up to this event prevented widespread travel problems.  However, ice accumulations on trees and power lines were significant.  Mercer county bore the brunt of the ice accumulations with nearly 7000 residents in Mercer county left without power during the height of the ice storm.  The weight of the ice toppled dozens of trees across northern Mercer county and at one point there were more than two dozen secondary roads closed from downed trees.  Outages in northern Mercer county lasted two to three days at least.  Monroe county fared the best with less than 100 residents suffering from power outages.||Here are some of the snow, sleet, and freezing rain accumulations reported across southeast West Virginia as of the morning of December 9, 2013.||Greenbrier (3N Lewisburg) - 0.50 inch sleet, 0.20 inch ice,|               (Clintonville) - 0.25 inch ice,|               (Smoot) - 0.25 inch,|Mercer (Athens) - 1.0 inch of sleet, 0.25 to 0.50 inch ice,|          (Flat Top) - 0.25 to 0.50 inch ice,|Monroe (4WNW Union) - 0.50 inch sleet, 0.25 inch ice,|           (2E Wolfcreek) - 0.25 inch ice,|           (1 SSE Alderson) - 0.25 inch ice,|Summers (Pipestem) - 0.50 inch sleet, 0.38 inch ice,|            (Talcott) - 0.25 inch ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SkyWarn spotter measured 1/4 inch ice accumulation from Clintonville in western Greenbrier county.  The COOP Observer, located at the Greenbrier Valley Airport just north of Lewisburg in Maxwelton also measured 1/4 inch ice accumulation as well as an additional 1/2 inch of snow.  Finally, a SkyWarn spotter also measured 1/4 inch ice accumulation in Smoot, about halfway between Lewisburg and Rainelle.  Over 1000 electric customers, mainly in western Greenbrier county, were left without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Appalachian Electric Power Company reported that over 7000 residents of northern Mercer county suffered extended power outages, in some cases lasting up to three to four days, as a result of 1/4 to 1/2 inch ice accumulations on trees and power lines.  Ice accumulations up to 1/4 inch were reported from Athens and Flat Top.  The SkyWarn spotter in Athens also reported one inch of sleet as well.  Dozens of trees were down across northern Mercer county and over two dozen roads were at least temporarily closed as a result of the downed trees, especially across the northern half of the county.
