EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast. Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon. Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations. Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area. ||The following is a list of snowfall reports from across West Virginia. ||- Greenbrier County - |4 SE Ronceverte: 8.2 inches, |Organ Cave: 7.0 inches, |2 NNE Dickson: 7.0 inches, |Lewisburg: 5.0 inches, |1 ENE Ronceverte: 3.0 inches, |White Sulphur Spring: 3.0 inches, |Alderson: 2.5 inches. ||- Mercer County - |Princeton: 15.0 inches, |South Bluefield: 11.8 inches, |Lilly Grove: 11.0 inches, |Bluefield: 10.0 inches, |Athens: 9.5 inches, |1 S South Bluefield: 6.0 inches. ||- Monroe County - |1 SSW Rock Camp: 12.0 inches, |Greenville: 12.0 inches, |Ballard: 12.0 inches, |1 SE Gap Mills: 12.0 inches, |1 NE Wayside: 12.0 inches, |5 W Union: 10.2 inches, |Pickaway: 9.5 inches, |Peterstown: 7.0 inches, |2 E Wolfcreek: 5.5 inches. ||- Summers County - |Hinton: 10.0 inches, |Wiggins: 7.0 inches, |1 NE Pipestem: 5.5 inches, |Pence Springs: 3.5 inches, |Sandstone: 0.7 inches, |Nimitz: 0.4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts across Summers County ranged from 10.0 inches at Hinton to less than a half inch at Nimitz. Other storm total snowfall reports include 7.0 inches at Wiggins and 5.5 inches at 1 NE Pipestem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In response to a strong mid level circulation, a low pressure strengthened as it moved up the Ohio River Valley during the daylight hours on the 5th.  It spread some light rain into southern West Virginia.   With an easterly flow into the mountains, boundary layer temperatures were colder in the central mountains and northern lowlands.||As the low center moves to near Point Pleasant on the evening of the 5th, snow developed over the central mountains.  A secondary low pressure developed in North Carolina,  The 2 systems consolidated over the Virginia coast by dawn on the 6th.||The snow diminished by midday on the 6th, with flurries and drizzle lingering into the night.   Accumulations of 5 to 12 inches were common in about 18 hours throughout Pocahontas and Randolph Counties.   At Snowshoe, the snow depth increased from 12 inches prior to the storm, to 22 inches.  The combination of an old snow cover and this storm also left Kumbrabow State Forest with a total snow depth of 22 inches. ||Webster County had lesser amounts, ranging from 2 inches in Webster Springs to 8 inches on Point Mountain.   Even lower amounts, mostly 1 to 3 inches, fell near Clarksburg, Grafton, Philippi, and Buckhannnon.    ||The heavy wet snow caused tree branches to fall onto power lines in Pocahontas County.  Roughly 3,100 customers in Pocahontas County lost electricity on the 6th.  Over 24 hours after the storm had ended, roughly half of those affected were still without electricity.  A warming shelter was set up in Green Bank by the county.

A storm system moved northeast out of south central Kentucky during the afternoon of the 24th.  The low pressure system reached northeast Ohio on the morning of the 25th.  Snow began during midday on the 24th in Pocahontas County on a cooling upslope wind from the southeast.  The snow spread north into the northern lowlands during the afternoon.  A lull occurred during the late afternoon and early evening.   An additional shot of snow was mixed with rain during the evening before changing over to frequent snow showers during the morning of the 25th.  ||Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were common by dawn on the 25th, north of the Route 50 corridor in the northern lowlands, including the Clarksburg vicinity.  Despite starting mostly as rain, a 2 to 3 inch accumulation of snow was also observed over the Barbour, Upshur, Nicholas, Fayette, and Raleigh by dawn on the 25th.   Five to 8 inches were common by dawn on the 25th in Pocahontas, Webster, and Randolph Counties.  Snow showers continued to accumulate in the mountain counties into the afternoon on the 26th with another 3 to 8 inches of accumulation. The snow depth at Snowshoe went from 4 inches prior to this episode to 15 inches on the 26th. By dawn on the 27th, Snowshoe snow depth was 20 inches.  In the lower terrain of these mountainous counties, a snow depth of 4 to 8 inches was common at dawn on the 26th. ||Meanwhile, after having rain on the 24th, Wyoming and McDowell Counties finally saw 1 to 4 inches of snow accumulation from the 25th into the 26th.

Just ahead of a squall line, temperatures were in the upper 60s to lower 70s.  Dew points were in the low and mid 50s.  The convective squall line moved east at about 35 to 40 mph, causing widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.  A few enhanced areas received severe convective wind gusts.||With strong dynamics aloft, widespread rain fell behind the squall line until the cold front passed on either side of 0000E on the 31st.  Rain amounts of 1 to 2 inches were common, with 2.5 inches in the Greenbrier Valley.  For example, Marlinton had 2.6 inches of rain.|    |As the prolonged rain was coming to an end, many small streams and creeks ran around bankfull late on the 30th.  This caused some minor water problems.  A woman drowned in Mason County, when she tried to crossed a swollen small stream.    ||Strong rises also occurred on the headwater rivers.  Some minor river flooding occurred.  The Greenbrier River crested just over 11 feet in Marlinton near dawn on the 31st.  Bankfull at Marlinton is 10 feet. This caused some minor flooding around houses near the mouth of Knapp Creek.  The Tug Fork River crested slightly above its 27 foot flood stage on the 31st during the early afternoon.  The crest was around 28.3 feet.  The  Tygart Valley River overflowed around Mill Creek and Belington.  The last effects of this heavy rain event was high water along the Ohio River that spilled over into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down from the Raccoon Creek area to around Milton.

An additional shot of snow was mixed with rain during the evening before changing over to frequent snow showers during the morning of the 25th.  ||Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were common by dawn on the 25th, north of the Route 50 corridor in the northern lowlands, including the Clarksburg vicinity.  Despite starting mostly as rain, a 2 to 3 inch accumulation of snow was also observed over the Barbour, Upshur, Nicholas, Fayette, and Raleigh by dawn on the 25th.   Five to 8 inches were common by dawn on the 25th in Pocahontas, Webster, and Randolph Counties.  Snow showers continued to accumulate in the mountain counties into the afternoon on the 26th with another 3 to 8 inches of accumulation. The snow depth at Snowshoe went from 4 inches prior to this episode to 15 inches on the 26th. By dawn on the 27th, Snowshoe snow depth was 20 inches.  In the lower terrain of these mountainous counties, a snow depth of 4 to 8 inches was common at dawn on the 26th. ||Meanwhile, after having rain on the 24th, Wyoming and McDowell Counties finally saw 1 to 4 inches of snow accumulation from the 25th into the 26th.

Just ahead of a squall line, temperatures were in the upper 60s to lower 70s.  Dew points were in the low and mid 50s.  The convective squall line moved east at about 35 to 40 mph, causing widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.  A few enhanced areas received severe convective wind gusts.||With strong dynamics aloft, widespread rain fell behind the squall line until the cold front passed on either side of 0000E on the 31st.  Rain amounts of 1 to 2 inches were common, with 2.5 inches in the Greenbrier Valley.  For example, Marlinton had 2.6 inches of rain.|    |As the prolonged rain was coming to an end, many small streams and creeks ran around bankfull late on the 30th.  This caused some minor water problems.  A woman drowned in Mason County, when she tried to crossed a swollen small stream.    ||Strong rises also occurred on the headwater rivers.  Some minor river flooding occurred.  The Greenbrier River crested just over 11 feet in Marlinton near dawn on the 31st.  Bankfull at Marlinton is 10 feet. This caused some minor flooding around houses near the mouth of Knapp Creek.  The Tug Fork River crested slightly above its 27 foot flood stage on the 31st during the early afternoon.  The crest was around 28.3 feet.  The  Tygart Valley River overflowed around Mill Creek and Belington.  The last effects of this heavy rain event was high water along the Ohio River that spilled over into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Tug Fork River, causing some road closures.  The crest in Williamson was just over 28 feet by 1400E of the 31st.  Flood stage is 27 feet, but a flood wall protects the town itself.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast. Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon. Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations. Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area. ||The following is a list of snowfall reports from across West Virginia. ||- Greenbrier County - |4 SE Ronceverte: 8.2 inches, |Organ Cave: 7.0 inches, |2 NNE Dickson: 7.0 inches, |Lewisburg: 5.0 inches, |1 ENE Ronceverte: 3.0 inches, |White Sulphur Spring: 3.0 inches, |Alderson: 2.5 inches. ||- Mercer County - |Princeton: 15.0 inches, |South Bluefield: 11.8 inches, |Lilly Grove: 11.0 inches, |Bluefield: 10.0 inches, |Athens: 9.5 inches, |1 S South Bluefield: 6.0 inches. ||- Monroe County - |1 SSW Rock Camp: 12.0 inches, |Greenville: 12.0 inches, |Ballard: 12.0 inches, |1 SE Gap Mills: 12.0 inches, |1 NE Wayside: 12.0 inches, |5 W Union: 10.2 inches, |Pickaway: 9.5 inches, |Peterstown: 7.0 inches, |2 E Wolfcreek: 5.5 inches. ||- Summers County - |Hinton: 10.0 inches, |Wiggins: 7.0 inches, |1 NE Pipestem: 5.5 inches, |Pence Springs: 3.5 inches, |Sandstone: 0.7 inches, |Nimitz: 0.4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts across Greenbrier County ranged from 8.2 inches at 4 SE Ronceverte to 2.5 inches at Alderson. Other storm total snowfall reports include 7.0 inches at Organ Cave, 7.0 inches at 2 NNE Dickson, 5.0 inches at Lewisburg, 3.0 inches at 1 ENE Ronceverte and 3.0 inches at White Sulphur Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted trough of low pressure moved through the Mid Atlantic while a weak clipper system moved through Central Virginia.  Cold temperatures and banding produced advisory level snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around seven inches were reported at Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North of a strong east to west front, a narrow band of snow streaked into the central West Virginia mountains around 0800E on the 17th.  Two to 5 inches of snow fell over southern Upshur County, Randolph County and the northern half of Pocahontas County by 1800E.  For example, Bartow in northern Pocahontas County reported 4 to 5 inches of snow accumulation.  At the extreme highest elevations, the maximum snow accumulation was around 6 inches, including the Snowshoe vicinity.  In the western lowlands, an inch or 2 of snow managed to accumulate in a few counties, including Mason, Jackson, and Roane.||An increasing flow from the east and southeast, causing lingering sleet, freezing rain, and freezing drizzle over eastern Randolph and Pocahontas County through the morning of the 18th.  Only a thin film of ice occurred.

A secondary low pressure system formed over the Carolinas as the primary system moved northward and was located across the Central Plains/Ohio Valley.  High pressure over New England and northerly winds brought below freezing temperatures across the Potomac Highlands.  Over running precipitation was able to melt and re-freeze on contact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice amounts of around a quarter inch were reported at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection broke out during the afternoon, ahead of a cold front moving southeast through Ohio.  The showers and thunderstorms moved southeast into West Virginia during the evening.  As the convection matured, some stronger wind gusts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gusted to 61 mph.  A few trees and large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection broke out during the afternoon, ahead of a cold front moving southeast through Ohio.  The showers and thunderstorms moved southeast into West Virginia during the evening.  As the convection matured, some stronger wind gusts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the electric box on the side of a house.  A fire was ignited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough was located across the eastern United States.  Northwest flow from a cold air mass caused upslope snow showers to occur across the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around five inches was reported at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough was located across the eastern United States.  Northwest flow from a cold air mass caused upslope snow showers to occur across the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around 3 inches was reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In an environment loaded with moisture, short lines and clusters of thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon.  ||One heavier cell passed across the Interstate 64 bridge over the Kanawha River near Nitro around 1630E.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shortly after a local downpour, 38 vehicles were involved in a chain reaction accident on the east bound lanes of the Interstate 64 Nitro Bridge over the Kanawha River.  Witnesses said there was standing water on the bridge.  There were about 7 or 8 vehicles in the middle of the pile-up that were not damaged.  Luckily, there were no fatalities.  However, 9 people were taken to the hospital, 2 in serious condition.  The interstate was shut down until around 2100E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located across the Great Lakes with an associated cold front extending from it.  Pre-frontal snow showers occurred in the higher elevations including Pendleton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around three inches were reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system that developed over the Ohio Valley and moved northeastward brought mixed precipitation areas along the mountains in Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. While strong isentropic lift provided for mainly rain over much of the region, cold air damming along the mountains allowed for surface temperatures to remain below freezing. A tenth to one quarter of an inch of ice was reported in Fayette county in PA,  Preston and Tucker counties in WV, and Garrett county MD.

A deepening low pressure system that developed over the Ohio Valley and moved northeastward brought mixed precipitation areas along the mountains in Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. While strong isentropic lift provided for mainly rain over much of the region, cold air damming along the mountains allowed for surface temperatures to remain below freezing. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the southern Ohio Valley before transitioning to a secondary low over northern NC. A very strong upper level disturbance diving in from the NW interacted with this second low causing it to rapidly deepen as it drifted northeast over eastern Virginia. Mid-level blocking to the north of this system caused it to slow down with strong Atlantic inflow into the mountains. As this low became the dominant feature a large swath of moderate to heavy snow developed and persisted especially north of I-64 with embedded banding bringing 1-2 per hour rates in spots. Snowfall ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reported at 7 inches in Alta and 6 inches at Lewisburg and Ronceverte. Electricity was knocked out around 230 AM in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved off the coast while low pressure approached from the southeast over the Carolinas.  Moisture streamed in from the south while temperatures were below or near freezing.  A wintry mix fell in areas along and west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of a trace was estimated at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well ahead of an approaching cold front in lower Ohio Valley, fast moving showers and thunderstorms streaked into western West Virginia from eastern Kentucky and  southeast Ohio during the afternoon.  Maximum wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were on the leading edge of the largest cluster of convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A partially rotten tree fell onto a vehicle on 12th Avenue causing significant damage.  Another tree fell near the Guyandotte boat ramp and damaged 3 vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As had been typical through much of the later half of the winter season across the eastern U.S., unseasonably cold air was in place across the region once again.  Morning low temperatures on this day were in the 20s across southeast West Virginia, with even teens in the mountains.  Needless to say, unseasonably cold for early April.  Meanwhile, a strong upper-level low pressure area in the southern stream was moving toward the region from the Mid-South.  A cold front was stalled along the Georgia and South Carolina Coast with a wave of surface low pressure tracking northeastward along the front near the Georgia Coast.  Precipitation began to spread into the area from the southwest during the mid-afternoon and quickly changed to sleet and then snow as a result of rapid dynamic cooling.  While the heaviest snow with this event passed just to the south across the New River Valley and adjacent areas of southwest Virginia, southeast West Virginia was not left out of the event.  Most of the four southeast West Virginia counties in the Blacksburg NWS County Warning Area (CWA) received on average one to three inches of snow.  Specifically, the following snow amounts were reported.||Greenbrier County - < 1/4 inch in Rainelle to 2 1/2 inches in Renick,|Mercer County - 2.0 inches in Princeton to 3.0 inches in Bluefield,|Monroe County - 1.0 inch in Union to 2.0 inches in Peterstown,|Summers County - 1.0 inch in Hinton to 3.0 inches in Hix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greenbrier County Emergency Management Agency along with the Broadcast Media reported snowfall ranging from less than 1/4 inch in the western part of the county near Rainelle to 2 1/2 inches in the northern part of the county near Renick.  Most of the county averaged 1 to 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along a stationary front south of the Washington DC.  Surface temperatures were marginal and snowfall accumulated west of the I-95 corridor.  A cold air damming situation formed during the event and led to accumulating snow across the Shenandoah Valley and Central Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 4.0 inches near Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The gradient between building high pressure form the west and low pressure lifting off to the northeast brought high winds to Greenbrier County. Over a dozen reports of tree and powerline damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a dozen reports of tree and powerline damage were received from scattered locations across the county. A wind gust of 51 mph was measured at Greenbrier County Airport at 535 PM, but substantially higher winds very likely occurred on exposed ridgelines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 17th.  This was well east of the much larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms over Kentucky.||In the humid air and with mid level support, that larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms surrvived overnight and crossed through West Virginia during the morning and early afternoon of the 18th.  Street flooding was reported in various spots.  Due to the wetter ground, minor flash flooding occurred in a portion of Roane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches in about 2 hours caused small streams to overflow onto roads and into yards.  The flooding was minor in comparison to the flash flooding that occurred on the 13th.  The automatic gauge near Spencer measured around 1.3 inches.  The cooperative observer in Spencer had 1.48 inches over 24 hours.||The Right Fork of Spring Creek closed Route 119 near the high school.  Laurel Run flooded portions of the Arnoldsburg Road or Routes 119 and 33. Goff Run flooded and damaged the road.  No evacuations occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating occurred in a moist and unstable environment, well ahead of an approaching cold front in the lower Ohio Valley.  Thunderstorms developed in West Virginia during the afternoon.  A few storms pulsed briefly to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in a mobile home park near Marina Park and into a housing development near Marina Drive.  The peak wind gust was estimated around 75 mph.  Several large trees were uprooted or blown down. Two mobile homes were destroyed by fallen trees with 5 others damaged.   A vehicle was turned on its side by an uprooted tree. Another vehicle was damaged by a fallen tree.  Two residents were taken to the hospital with minor injures and later released.  A small private cemetery had 2 toppled trees.  The exposed root systems dislodged several headstones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary moved northward and stalled over the Mid-Atlantic/I-95 corridor overnight. An upper level low approached the region and a upper level jet added to enhanced lift in the region. Heavy rain caused a lot of flash flooding with amounts up to seven inches in less than 6 hours recorded near Washington Dulles Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were three to four feet of water ponded on Durgon Road near Creitz Road.  There was also a disabled vehicle in water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of convection crossed the Ohio River into West Virginia during the evening hours of the 9th.  Northern portions of Tyler County had already received 1 to 2 inches of rain during the early morning hours.  Even further east, the ground prior to the rain was much wetter than normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1 to 1.5 inches in less than 6 hours had small streams flooding roads.  One example was Shinns Run over Saltwell Road. Water was getting into some garages in Shinnston.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 9 inches were reported at Sulphur City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eastern fringe of a deep moist southern flow, north to south bands of thunderstorms during the late afternoon of southern West Virginia.  They formed on a west to east thermal gradient. Temperatures reached the mid 80s around Charleston, while 70s prevailed under more layered clouds further to the west. ||Thunderstorms pulsed to severe limits in the eastern section of Charleston, on north toward Pinch and Elkview.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams flooded roads.  In the town of Paden City 2 feet of flowing water was observed.  Sugarcamp Run also flooded.  On Starkey Ridge the rain total from the 2 rounds of showers and thunderstorms was 2.3 inches in less than 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms formed in the afternoon from an approaching disturbance aloft and its associated mid level cooling.  The showers became oriented west to east, causing some repetitive showers from near Bridgeport of Harrison County on east to near Grafton of Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in 3 hours over wet terrain.  Small feeder streams flooded roads.  One example was Davisson Run, which flows into Simpson Creek.  The small stream flooded Route 50 in Bridgeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms formed in the afternoon from an approaching disturbance aloft and its associated mid level cooling.  The showers became oriented west to east, causing some repetitive showers from near Bridgeport of Harrison County on east to near Grafton of Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of mostly 1.5 to 2 inches fell in less than 3 hours over wet terrain.  A gauge at Grafton measured 2.28 inches in 24 hours.  Minor flooding occurred when small feeder streams overflowed onto roads.  One example, a feeder run into Three Fork Creek flowed across County Route 52 in Thornton.  County Route 46 near Knottsville flooded. No dwellings were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level disturbance in the winds aloft caused showers and thunderstorms to form and expand over southern West Virginia as the new month began.  The convection moved north and northwest.  Movement was only around 15 to 20 mph and repetitive cells affected small portions of Wayne County.||Another round of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through the western counties of West Virginia during that evening.  An arch of convection came out of eastern Kentucky and joined forces with another area of showers moving north off the western slopes of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell and damaged a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eastern edge of thick south to north cloud shield, bands of showers and thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repetitive showers and thunderstorms caused around 1.5 inches of rain to fall  in less than 2 hours in narrow south to north bands.  This caused the usual street flooding and ponding of water in poor drainage areas.  The Camden Park Amusement Park had 4 to 6 inches of water collect in spots during the downpour.||The sewage plant in West Huntington reported 2.3 inches of rain in 24 hours.  The Tri State Airport measured 1.43 inches in less than 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level disturbance in the winds aloft caused showers and thunderstorms to form and expand over southern West Virginia as the new month began.  The convection moved north and northwest.  Movement was only around 15 to 20 mph and repetitive cells affected small portions of Wayne County.||Another round of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through the western counties of West Virginia during that evening.  An arch of convection came out of eastern Kentucky and joined forces with another area of showers moving north off the western slopes of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in about 90 minutes over wet terrain.  The airport measured 1.32 inches between 2100E and 2200E.  ||Pond Creek flooded roads in the southern portion of the county.  Water was up to the headlights on vehicles in the streets of Parkersburg.  Water backed up from streets into some homes in the Mt Pleasants Estates area of Mineralwells, plus within the city of Parkersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the winds aloft passed during the monring.  Clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved east across West Virginia at about 25 to 30 mph.  Repetitive downpours occurred for about an hour over a small portion of southern Clay County near the Kanawha county border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated rain of 2 to 3 inches in just over an hour flooded small streams in a small portion of southern Clay County.  Not too far away in Kanawha County, one gauge measured 1.74 inches in less than an hour.  ||The headwaters of Blue Creek and Sycamore Creek flooded.  This includes Adonijah Fork and Laurel Fork.  The Right Fork of Sycamore Creek flooded roads around Lizemores.  The rapid runoff around a general store got a few inches of water inside.  Nearby, a church near the junction of Laurel Fork and Adonijah Fork, on Independence Road, had 2 feet of water surrounding the building.  Around 50 parishioners had to wait about an hour for the water levels to recede before leaving the church grounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving convection during the early evening of the 19th dumped 1 to 2.5 inches of rain over a small area along the Dry Fork between Bradshaw and English.  ||A weak mid level flow existed over a moist lower atmosphere.  Slow moving showers, with embedded thunderstorms, redeveloped after dawn on the 20th over McDowell County.  These showers moved along their northwest to southeast axis, causing repetitive showers over the small streams during the mid and late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of around an inch over saturated ground caused minor flash flooding.  Water was over low spots along roads. The road along Cow Hollow near Bartley was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region and showers and thunderstorms developed.  Ahead of cold front instability and shear led to isolated severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees down on Cacapon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large north to south band of showers and thunderstorms moved slowly east during the morning hours on the 7th.  By midday, the eastern edge of this cluster increased in intensity as it moved through Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Tygart Lake State Park.  No damaged to structures occurred, but roads were blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large north to south band of showers and thunderstorms moved slowly east during the morning hours on the 7th.  By midday, the eastern edge of this cluster increased in intensity as it moved through Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, some onto power lines.  A few power poles were also damaged.  County Route 7 known as Irontown Road was blocked by debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heating combined with weak disturbance in the southerly winds aloft to trigger a few thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1 to 2 inches fell in less than 90 minutes in a narrow corridor around St Albans and Dunbar.  One spotter on Vine Street in St Albans measured a 24 hour total of 2.03 inches of rain. Not far away, the St Albans cooperative observer had only 0.8 inches.||Along the valley floor section of St Albans considerable street flooding occurred.  Clogged drains compounded the problem.  On Monmouth Street the water got inside some parked cars.  Due to the recent wet pattern, water even ponded in yards and gardens.  Water drained into several basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a cold front, showers and thunderstorms survived during the late night or early morning hours.  Frequent lightning and brief downpours were common.  A few storms pulsed to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to around 45 mph caused trees to fall onto power lines in East Bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a cold front, showers and thunderstorms survived during the late night or early morning hours.  Frequent lightning and brief downpours were common.  A few storms pulsed to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.  One large evergreen tree fell and blocked a road.  Wind gusts were estimated around 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the humidity of a waning heat wave, clusters of showers and thunderstorms starting forming by midday in West Virginia on the 19th.||Their outflow boundaries, along with differential heating and a mid level disturbance, helped develop other areas of showers and thunderstorm into the evening.||Downpours and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains estimated at 2 to 3 inches in less than 2 hours caused small streams to flood roads.  One example was Stewart Run. It flooded and damaged a portion of County Route 10/2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong summer disturbance in the winds aloft moved up the Ohio Valley during the morning.  It combined with the muggy air to produce local downpours during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 year old man drowned, after falling into Bear Branch, a small tributary to Eighteen Mile Creek east of Buffalo. He was removing debris from the stream around a bridge.  The creek was swollen from over an inch of rain during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level low developed over West Virginia on the 12th, then drifted into southeast Ohio by that evening.  This stopped the expected dry air intrusion along the Ohio Valley.  The low level flow brought an increase in moisture again from the east.||Rain showers developed over the mountainous counties by midday.  Plenty of sunshine in the western lowlands increased instability.  Clusters of convection developed during the afternoon with a slow west or southwest drift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1 to 1.75 inches fell over wet terrain in about an hour.  This caused small streams in extreme southern Roane County to flood. ||Green Creek flooded, especially its Left Fork.  Roads were flooded and driveways were blocked.  One truck was swept downstream.  The vehicle was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the humidity of a waning heat wave, clusters of showers and thunderstorms starting forming by midday in West Virginia on the 19th.||Their outflow boundaries, along with differential heating and a mid level disturbance, helped develop other areas of showers and thunderstorm into the evening.||Downpours and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell onto a roof of a house along Rich Fork Road.  The fallen tree had more to due with saturated soils then strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the humidity of a waning heat wave, clusters of showers and thunderstorms starting forming by midday in West Virginia on the 19th.||Their outflow boundaries, along with differential heating and a mid level disturbance, helped develop other areas of showers and thunderstorm into the evening.||Downpours and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer along Lockhart Fork measured 2.88 inches of rain in over 2 hours. ||The headwaters of the Left Fork of Sandy Creek flooded, including the intersection of Medina Road and Route 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the humidity of a waning heat wave, clusters of showers and thunderstorms starting forming by midday in West Virginia on the 19th.||Their outflow boundaries, along with differential heating and a mid level disturbance, helped develop other areas of showers and thunderstorm into the evening.||Downpours and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After earlier rains in the day, another round of convection dropped 1 to 2 inches of rain in 3 hours.  This caused runoff from mountain slopes to get into a few houses in Ansted.  Street and poor drainage flooding also occurred around Hpoewell.  A vehicle got stuck in water along County Route 3/6 near Hico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level low developed over West Virginia on the 12th, then drifted into southeast Ohio by that evening.  This stopped the expected dry air intrusion along the Ohio Valley.  The low level flow brought an increase in moisture again from the east.||Rain showers developed over the mountainous counties by midday.  Plenty of sunshine in the western lowlands increased instability.  Clusters of convection developed during the afternoon with a slow west or southwest drift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving downpours caused streams to flood and block roads.||A spotter in Craigsville measured 2.42 inches of rain, while another spotter near Runa measured 2.37 inches.      ||For example, Route 55 south of Craigsville was flooded with water 6 to 12 inches deep.  Meadow Creek had several feet of water over Runa Road.  Muddlety Creek also flooded roads.||There was no damage to structures reported to the county emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eastern fringe of a deep moist southern flow, north to south bands of thunderstorms during the late afternoon of southern West Virginia.  They formed on a west to east thermal gradient. Temperatures reached the mid 80s around Charleston, while 70s prevailed under more layered clouds further to the west. ||Thunderstorms pulsed to severe limits in the eastern section of Charleston, on north toward Pinch and Elkview.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large branches were blown down.  Some power lines were brought down by the trees.

Temperatures reached the mid 80s around Charleston, while 70s prevailed under more layered clouds further to the west. ||Thunderstorms pulsed to severe limits in the eastern section of Charleston, on north toward Pinch and Elkview.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches fell.

With a front stalled to the east and south, waves of low pressure caused periods of rain for 2 days.   Rain totals of 1.5 to 2 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The headwaters of Paint Creek overflowed and blocked roads near Pax and Weirwood.  Access to a few homes was limited.  No dwellings were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a front stalled to the east and south, waves of low pressure caused periods of rain for 2 days.   Rain totals of 1.5 to 2 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The headwaters of the Tygart Valley River rose above the 11 foot bankfull level, cresting around 12.1 feet during the afternoon of the 16th.  A few country roads were flooded.  No dwellings were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm air, with afternoon temperatures reaching into the 60s and low 70s, and a surge of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with a slow moving cold front during the afternoon and evening hours of January 30th, resulting in strong low-topped showers and thunderstorms and widespread heavy rainfall. Despite weak CAPE associated with the frontal passage, low level wind shear was in excess of 60 knots across the region, which made for strong squall lines of shower activity, often with strong winds but little to no lightning. Precipitable water values were observed to be in the 1.0 to 1.5 inch range, which is extremely high for January across the Appalachians and the nearby Piedmont region. To add to the flooding threat, ground conditions were already saturated from recent rain and snow during the days and weeks preceding the event.||The following is a list of rainfall totals, in inches, that fell across portions of southeast West Virginia:|| - Greenbrier County - |    2.45   McRoss 3 E COOP,|    2.36   4 SE Ronceverte CCR,|    2.05   Lewisburg 3 N COOP,|    2.04   Williamsburg IFLOWS,|    1.96   Alderson IFLOWS,|    1.92   Meadow Bluff IFLOWS,|    1.90   White Sulphur Springs DCP,|    1.86   White Sulphur Springs COOP,|    1.63   Brushy Mountain IFLOWS.|| - Mercer County - |    2.08   Bluefield COOP,|    1.94   Eads Mill IFLOWS,|    1.91   Egeria IFLOWS,|    1.79   Bluefield IFLOWS,|    1.74   Bluefield FAA Airport,|    1.72   Arista IFLOWS,|    1.67   Mataoka IFLOWS,|    1.55   Bramwell IFLOWS.|| - Monroe County - |    2.50   Rock Camp DCP,|    1.40   Ballard IFLOWS,|    1.39   Union IFLOWS.|| - Summers County - |    2.18   Bluestone River at Pipestem,|    2.14   Pipestem RAWS,|    2.13   Pipestem Knob IFLOWS,|    2.10   Alderson,|    2.04   Keeney Knob IFLOWS,|    1.95   Bluestone Lake COOP.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East River at Willowton Road rose to flood stage due to prolonged heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was slow to move off the Atlantic Coast and sub freezing air was trapped on the leeward side of the Appalachian Mountains.  Southwest flow a few thousand feet above the surface brought above freezing temperatures and led to freezing rain and accumulating ice in the Mid Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of around a trace was reported at Keyser and surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted trough of low pressure moved through the Mid Atlantic while a weak clipper system moved through Central Virginia.  Cold temperatures and banding produced advisory level snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 inches were reported at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West Virginia was well entrenched in cold arctic air.  Temperatures were mostly in the upper teens and lower 20s during the afternoon of the 23rd.  A warm front developed in Kentucky.  A narrow, but elongated west to east, band of light snow developed and lifted northeast into central West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  An occluded front passed overnight.  The snow became lingering snow showers Thursday morning on the 24th.  Snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches in 12 to 18 hours were widespread across Randolph and Webster Counties.   The deepest snow accumulations came from the Helvetia, Pickens and Kumbrabow State Forest vicinity.  Snow accumulations of 12 to 13 inches were measured there.||During the same time interval, snow accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were common across Barbour, Upshur, Pocahontas, Lewis, Nicholas, and Braxton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around two inches were reported at Bunker Hill and surrounding locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around three to four inches were reported at Pendleton and surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain showers began during the warm afternoon on the 5th, ahead of an approaching cold front.  In the wake of the frontal passage during that evening. the showers transformed into periods of rain.  The cold rain continued into the evening hours of the 6th as a low pressure wave rippled along the front.||Rains of 2 to 3 inches over 24 to 30 hours caused small streams and creeks to overflow in the usual vulnerable spots by the evening twilight on the 6th.  Minor river flooding even occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams overflowed and closed roads.  Later, the Elk River crested at Clay around 19.45 feet during the early morning hours on the 7th.  Flood stage is 18 feet at Clay.  The crest of about a foot over flood stage did not reach Queen Shoals and the Kanawha County border until just after dawn.  Some outbuildings and summer camps were flooded.  Fencing was damaged.||This was the first flood along the lower Elk River, though minor, in 10 years.  The flood of November 2003 was more severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain showers began during the warm afternoon on the 5th, ahead of an approaching cold front.  In the wake of the frontal passage during that evening. the showers transformed into periods of rain.  The cold rain continued into the evening hours of the 6th as a low pressure wave rippled along the front.||Rains of 2 to 3 inches over 24 to 30 hours caused small streams and creeks to overflow in the usual vulnerable spots by the evening twilight on the 6th.  Minor river flooding even occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small streams flooded and closed roads.  Later, the minor flooding along the Elk River and the swollen Coal River also kept the most vulnerable low spots closed into the midday hours on the 7th. A few basements were flooded in Clendenin when the Elk River was cresting during the morning on the 7th. A few vehicles parked on Thorofare Road in Clendenin were also flooded. Some outbuildings were also flooded along the Elk River. ||A few vehicles were damaged when they drove into flooded roads or underpasses and stalled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with record warmth in a large section of West Virginia.  Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s for much of the western lowlands. ||In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down east of the Interstate 79 including along Jesse Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another fast moving clipper system brought mostly 2 to 3 inches of snow to northern counties of West Virginia during about a 6 hour period during the late afternoon and  evening. Travel delays were widespread on area roads during the early evening.

Rain showers began during the warm afternoon on the 5th, ahead of an approaching cold front.  In the wake of the frontal passage during that evening. the showers transformed into periods of rain.  The cold rain continued into the evening hours of the 6th as a low pressure wave rippled along the front.||Rains of 2 to 3 inches over 24 to 30 hours caused small streams and creeks to overflow in the usual vulnerable spots by the evening twilight on the 6th.  Minor river flooding even occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams, including the headwaters of the Birch River, flooded roads across northern sections of the county.

After a one day lull in the active weather on Saturday the 7th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain quickly spread back northeast into West Virginia by dawn on Sunday the 8th.||Strong low level warm air advection combined with support from a 170 knot upper jet maximum to enhance the initial shot of snow in a narrow southwest to northeast band across the heart of West Virginia.  About a 40 to 50 mile wide corridor from near Huntington, on northeast through Winfield, Spencer, Glenville, Weston, Buckhannon, Elkins, and Philippi saw mostly 4 to 6 inches of snow accumulate in 6 hours or less.  Randolph County had 4 to 8 inches depending on elevation.  Just north and south of that band, snow amounts were less, mostly 1 to 3 inches.||The southern mountains including Fayette and Raleigh Counties had very little snow, starting mostly as sleet.  An increasing southeast wind caused surface temperatures to remain around 30 to 32, causing a period of freezing rain.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch were observed by midday. ||The precipitation decreased during the early afternoon as the support streaked toward the Mid Atlantic States.  Travel conditions improved during the afternoon.||Another batch of rain moved north during the evening of the 8th.  This renewed the freezing rain in portions of Fayette and Raleigh County.  The ice accretion reached a half inch around Cool Ridge in southern Raleigh County during the night. With slightly colder temperatures in the upper 20s to around 30 degrees, Pocahontas County received a quarter inch of ice during the evening, after receiving 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet during the day on the 8th.||In the higher terrain around Cool Ridge tree branches sagged, some snapped off. Power outages also occurred.  About 3,200 customers were reported without electricity in Raleigh County.

After a cold dawn, with temperatures on either side of zero, strong warm air advection developed light snow by midday on the 2nd.  One to 3 inches of snow was common into the evening hours.  After a late night lull, snow showers increased during the morning of the 3rd.  Mid level disturbances combined with plentiful low level moisture.  The snow showers decreased during the evening on the 4th.  In well over 24 hours, 4 to 8 inches of snow fell in the central and southern mountain counties with less in the lowlands.

A departing storm system along the east coast brought northwest flow snows across southeast West Virginia ranging from less than 1 inch up to 6 inches in the highest elevations. County-wide averages were generally in the 2 to 4 inch range and did not meet winter storm criteria. Roads became quite slippery however, and several significant accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 77 was closed for a period near mile marker 7 between Princeton and Bluefield|when two different vehicles lost control on the snowy roads. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Canadian cold front combined with a powerful mid-U.S. upper-level trough to bring a period of light snow to the mountains of eastern West Virginia on the 10th continuing into the early morning hours of the 11th.  While snow amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range across most of the southeast West Virginia counties within the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA), upslope enhanced snow showers in western Greenbrier county brought snowfall totals there into the three to four inch range with a maximum of six inches being recorded in the far northwest part of the county at Quinwood.  Monroe county, however, did not report any measurable snowfall.||Here are some of the snowfall totals received from the southeast West Virginia counties within the Blacksburg CWA.||Greenbrier (Quinwood) - 6.0 inches,|              (McRoss) - 4.0 inches,|              (Renick) - 2.8 inches,||Mercer (Princeton) - 1.5 inch,|          (Bluefield) - trace,||Summer (Meadow Creek) - 2.0 inches,|            (Hix) - 2.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greenbrier County Emergency Management Director reported snowfall ranging from 2.8 inches in Renick in the north central part of the county to 6.0 inches in Quinwood in the far northwest part of the county.  The McRoss COOP observer reported 4.0 inches in McRoss, about 10 miles southeast of Quinwood.

Upslope snow showers developed behind a strong cold front across portions of southeast West Virginia. Snow amounts were only in the 1 to 2 inch range but slick roadways were reported along with a few accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patches of black ice and snowy roads were reported on the Sam Black Church and Alta areas of the county with several accidents reported. Interstate 64 westbound was close for over an hour due to one accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper moved through the Mid Atlantic producing light snow for most of the region.  Dry air at the surface limited snowfall amounts for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around one inch was reported at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper moved through the Mid Atlantic producing light snow for most of the region.  Dry air at the surface limited snowfall amounts for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around two inches were reported at Harpers Ferry and surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was wedged down the east coast and surface temperatures fell below freezing. Low pressure developed across the gulf coast states and moved across the Ohio Valley. Snow began but changed over to sleet and freezing rain as warmer temperatures aloft overran the cold air at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of around six inches occurred at surrounding locations.

An upper air disturbance, combined with warm air advection, to cause 2 to 3 inches of snow accumulation in less than 8 hours.  Only an inch or two of snow fell further south and in the mountains.  Due to the cold temperatures, untreated surfaces were quickly covered, causing travel delays.  Many schools were dismissed early for the day.

Due to the cold temperatures, untreated surfaces were quickly covered, causing travel delays.  Many schools were dismissed early for the day.

An Arctic cold front moved through the region accompanied by a deep negatively titled upper trough from December 17th into the 18th.  This brought a period of upslope convective snow showers in discrete bands to the Alleghany Mountains of eastern West Virginia and far southwest Virginia.  Snow only fell for a brief period of time, but it fell heavily in these bands accumulating from two to four inches in less than six hours.  Even thundersnow was reported in Tazewell county, Virginia.  Snowfall totals were mostly in the one to three inch range.  However, the higher elevations of northwestern Greenbrier saw up to six inches of snow in a few spots.||Here are some snowfall totals observed in the eastern West Virginia counties within the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA).||Greenbrier (Quinwood) - 6.0 inches,|               (Droop Mountain) - 2.0 inches,|               (Rainelle) - 2.0 inches,|Mercer (Bluefield) - 0.5 inch,|Monroe (1NNW Maggie) - 4.0 inches,|           (1S Sweet Springs) - 4.0 inches,|Summers (Hix) - 1.0 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Virginia Department of Highways reported snowfall ranging from 2.0 inches in Rainelle to 6.0 inches in Quinwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled to the south of the Mid Atlantic region and a weak disturbance  moved along it. Northerly flow caused temperatures to get below freezing west of the Blue Ridge and Northern Maryland. Moisture and warmer air aloft streamed in from the Atlantic and freezing rain formed where temperatures were below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice reports of less than a tenth inch were reported in surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled to the south of the Mid Atlantic region and a weak disturbance  moved along it. Northerly flow caused temperatures to get below freezing west of the Blue Ridge and Northern Maryland. Moisture and warmer air aloft streamed in from the Atlantic and freezing rain formed where temperatures were below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice reports of less than a tenth inch were reported in surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance, combined with warm air advection, to cause 2 to 3 inches of snow accumulation in less than 8 hours.  Only an inch or two of snow fell further south and in the mountains.  Due to the cold temperatures, untreated surfaces were quickly covered, causing travel delays.  Many schools were dismissed early for the day.

West Virginia was well entrenched in cold arctic air.  Temperatures were mostly in the upper teens and lower 20s during the afternoon of the 23rd.  A warm front developed in Kentucky.  A narrow, but elongated west to east, band of light snow developed and lifted northeast into central West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  An occluded front passed overnight.  The snow became lingering snow showers Thursday morning on the 24th.  Snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches in 12 to 18 hours were widespread across Randolph and Webster Counties.   The deepest snow accumulations came from the Helvetia, Pickens and Kumbrabow State Forest vicinity.  Snow accumulations of 12 to 13 inches were measured there.||During the same time interval, snow accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were common across Barbour, Upshur, Pocahontas, Lewis, Nicholas, and Braxton.

An Arctic cold front moved through the region accompanied by a deep negatively titled upper trough from December 17th into the 18th.  This brought a period of upslope convective snow showers in discrete bands to the Alleghany Mountains of eastern West Virginia and far southwest Virginia.  Snow only fell for a brief period of time, but it fell heavily in these bands accumulating from two to four inches in less than six hours.  Even thundersnow was reported in Tazewell county, Virginia.  Snowfall totals were mostly in the one to three inch range.  However, the higher elevations of northwestern Greenbrier saw up to six inches of snow in a few spots.||Here are some snowfall totals observed in the eastern West Virginia counties within the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA).||Greenbrier (Quinwood) - 6.0 inches,|               (Droop Mountain) - 2.0 inches,|               (Rainelle) - 2.0 inches,|Mercer (Bluefield) - 0.5 inch,|Monroe (1NNW Maggie) - 4.0 inches,|           (1S Sweet Springs) - 4.0 inches,|Summers (Hix) - 1.0 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SkyWarn Spotter observed 1/2 inch of snow in the Bluefield area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of around a trace was reported at surrounding locations.

A strengthening low pressure lifted north through the Chicago and the Lake Michigan vicinity during the late afternoon on Sunday the 17th.  A strong low level wind field existed in the Ohio Valley.  Extension clouds and showers fell during the day on Sunday well ahead of the associated cold front.  Surface surface dew points raised into the upper 50 degrees by nightfall on Sunday evening.  Dew points peaked at 60 degrees in southern West Virginia. ||As the parent low pressure center lifted north toward Ontario, the associated front swept into West Virginia during the night.  Narrow bands of convective showers preceded the front.  The showers were moving at 50 to 60 mph.  Widespread wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph occurred.  A few locations had severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were fell onto power lines.  Portions of Main and South Streets were blocked.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of 32 degrees or less were measured at Garrett County Airport and Petersburg Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong convergence of abundant tropical moisture along a warm front stretching from the Ohio Valley into southeast West Virginia triggered rounds of heavy rain. The NWS Blacksburg upper air sounding from the morning of the 20th, indicated precipitable water values were more than 2 standard deviations above normal across the region. Bands of rain passed repeatedly across the several of the same locations in Greenbrier and Summers Counties, resulting in 4 to 5 inches accumulation in some areas over a few hours time.  Heavy rain was confined to mainly a 15 to 20 mile wide corridor, with much lower amounts occurring outside this corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream flooding was observed in the community of Dawson, near exit 150 on Interstate 64, where six inches of water was reported flowing over Grassy Meadows Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest to southeast oriented warm front struggled to lift northeast in West Virginia from the 19th into the 20th.  Surface dew points were in the 65 to 70 degree range to the west of the boundary.  A narrow band of showers and thunderstorms, oriented along the front, trained over a portion of Nicholas County during the morning hours on the 20th.  To a lesser degree, the same warm front caused some high water in western Harrison County later that same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 2 to 3.5 inches fell in 3 to 5 hours early on Friday morning the 20th.  A spotter near Runa reported 3.25 inches of rain.  A gauge at Nallen measured around 3.2 inches of rain.  Its heaviest rain rate was .9 inches in 30 minutes.  Small streams flooded roads. Water flowed over small bridges.  One example was Laurel Branch, a tributary to Peters Creek.  It flooded Route 129.  On Peters Creek, the stream gauge near Lockwood rose around 6 feet in the vertical.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection formed during Labor Day afternoon. The air was warm and humid. A cold front was located over central Ohio.  The prefrontal convection was in several small clusters, not a solid line.||One batch of storms and showers became oriented west to east along the flow during the evening hours over Taylor County. The cooperative observer at the Tygart Lake Dam reported 1.42 inches of rain, most of which fell in an hour between 1900 and 2000E.  An automatic gauge near Grafton had 1.38 inches of rain in 30 minutes.  In an hour, 1.5 inches of rain was measured at that same gauge.  Higher amounts of 1.5 to 1.75 inches were estimated from dual-pol radar west of Tygart Lake in the Grafton and Prunytown areas.  Minor flooding occurred around Grafton and Prunytown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Otter Creek flowed out of its banks and flooded Route 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper trough was deepening across the central U.S.  A cold front was approaching southeast West Virginia from the west.  Very moist tropical air was present across the region once again as this event unfolded, with precipitable water values once again near 2.0 inches across the area.  Thunderstorms developed quickly during the afternoon across southwest Virginia and southeast West Virginia, especially in the Giles County, Virginia and Monroe County, West Virginia area.  The same thunderstorm that caused flash flooding in Rich Creek, Virginia also was responsible for flash flooding in Peterstown, along the West Virginia/Virginia state line.  U.S. Highway 219 was flooded through downtown Peterstown and several side streets and parking lots were flooded in the area as well.  Debris was washed into the highway at several locations in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WVVA Television of Bluefield, WV reported that rainfall of nearly three inches in the Peterstown area within two hours caused flash flooding, especially along U.S. Highway 219 from the Virginia state line northward to the north side of Peterstown.  Mud and debris were washed out onto the road at several locations.  In addition, several side streets and business parking lots along U.S. Highway 219 were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts estimated at 1.5 to 2 inches fell in about 3 hours.  Swollen streams and minor street flooding occurred in Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid environment existed over West Virginia with surface dew points in the lower 70s.  A weak steering flow existed.  Several small clusters of slow moving showers and embedded thunderstorms formed over West Virginia on the 8th, well south of a cold front across northern Ohio.||The front sagged slowly south into the 10th.  Another round of convection occurred near dawn on the 10th in the Huntington to Charleston corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of around 2.5 inches fell in less than 3 hours near dawn. Rainfall totals over 24 hours were over 3 inches.  Small seasonal tributary runs, plus Charley Creek, flooded yards and roads. Water was 1 to 2 feet over the roads in spots.  One house had basement flooding.   Minor damage to gravel driveways occurred.  Lawn furniture and a shed were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South of a cold front, mid level support and upper level jet dynamics helped form and enhance showers and thunderstorms in eastern Ohio during the late evening on the 12th.  These clusters trained east into north central West Virginia near the Route 50 corridor. In this maximum rainfall band, amounts were on the order of 2 to 4 inches from about 0000E to 0400E on the 13th.  Similar to Ohio, the greatest observed rainfall in West Virginia also occurred along the Ohio River.  A spotter in Williamstown measured 4.41 inches of rain, while further down the river a spotter in Vienna had 3.05 inches. In Pleasants County, an automatic gauge in Saint Marys measured 3.9 inches.  In southern Tyler County, another automatic gauge measured 3.5 inches at Conway Lake.  The gauges near Ellenboro and Cairo in Ritchie County measured between 1.5 to 2 inches, but amounts were likely heavier just to their north.  Heading further east in West Virginia, rain amounts gradually lowered.  The cooperative observer in West Union had 2.9 inches.  An automatic gauge in northern Harrison County along Little Ten Mile Creek near Brown had 2.59 inches.  Maximum rainfall in Taylor County was also around 2.5 inches.  A gauge along Laurel Run had 2.55 inches.||Small stream flooding was widespread in this west to east rainfall band.  In the most vulnerable spots, houses or mobile homes were damaged.  Several parked vehicles were flooded.  Damage occurred to roads and culverts.  Small private bridges were damaged.  A strong rise occurred on the Middle Island Creek in Tyler and Pleasants County.  The stream gauge near Little saw the water level increase 13 feet in the vertical.||Thankfully, no injuries or deaths occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One of the small streams that flooded was Missouri Run northwest of Mineral Wells.  A car was moved off the road along County Route 38/5.    Worthington Creek and Dry Run flooded roads.  Basement flooding occurred in Parkersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level trough persisted just to the west of the area yet another day.  A warm, humid air mass remained in place across the region along with numerous outflow boundaries from earlier convection.  Scattered bands of thunderstorms developed across the region throughout the afternoon, some repeatedly developing or training across the same areas.  One area that was subject to this was western Greenbrier county, where thunderstorms dumped two to three inches of rain in less than two hours causing flooding along Muddy Creek near Crawley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Virginia Department of Highways reported that Muddy Creek flooded two roads in western Greenbrier county.  Hamilton Bridge Road was flooded with water flowing over the road as a result of a backed up culvert or drainage pipe.  The road sustained minor damage as a result.  Blue Sulphur Springs Road was also flooded by the waters of Muddy Creek, but no damage was observed to this road.

Stronger wind gusts occurred in a few areas.    ||No injuries occurred.  Over 10,000 customers in Cabell County and over 8,000 customers in Wayne County were without electricity.  A few schools were closed for the day due to the power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered locations in the city had trees down, including around Ritter Park. A 300 year white oak tree fell along 11th Avenue.  The tree snapped and splintered at the trunk.  The female homeowner said, everyone understands, this is like a death in the family to me.  The fallen tree did cause some minor damaged to the roof of her house and broke a few windows too.  A roof on a 7th Avenue warehouse was damaged.  The wind blew through some broken windows to help lift a portion of the roof.  Also, a section of a side cinder block wall also fell.  Both the roof and the wall were not anchored well.  A tree fell onto power lines on 8th Avenue.  A trained spotted at Marshall University estimated the wind gusts at 65 to 70 mph with many large branches blown down.

The stream gauge near Little saw the water level increase 13 feet in the vertical.||Thankfully, no injuries or deaths occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along the North Fork of the Hughes River toward the community of Mountain, feeder streams flooded. Farm equipment and a barn were damaged by flood waters.  Bunnell Run flooded 3 or 4 houses and a car dealership around Pennsboro.  Other houses were surrounded by water. ||Near Ellenboro, 7 temporary mobile trailers and 5 vehicles in a recreational vehicle park were damaged by flooding along Hushers Run.  One resident described waking up and wading through 2 to 3 feet of water to reach safety.||Around 5 people were evacuated in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable northwest flow aloft persisted across the region thanks to an equally persistent strong subtropical ridge across the central U.S.  A strengthening upper level storm system was diving southeast from the Great Lakes.  The air mass across the region was unseasonably warm for late August, and as a result was quite unstable.  These factors combined to produce isolated severe thunderstorms.  The initial thunderstorms developed across far southwest Virginia and southeast West Virginia during the early to mid-afternoon as a result of outflow boundaries from overnight thunderstorms that moved through Kentucky, Tennessee and western West Virginia.  The thunderstorms propagated and developed eastward through the evening, impacting much of the Blacksburg, VA County Warning Area (CWA).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail along U.S. 219 at Spruce Run Road just north of downtown Lindside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong autumn cold front swept across between 0100E and 0400E.  Strong winds existed just above the surface.  There were fast moving showers near the front, with just a few embedded thunderstorms.  The convection was able to mix some of the strong wind gusts down to the surface.||Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common in West Virginia.  Stronger wind gusts occurred in a few areas.    ||No injuries occurred.  Over 10,000 customers in Cabell County and over 8,000 customers in Wayne County were without electricity.  A few schools were closed for the day due to the power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 61 mph was measured at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with record warmth in a large section of West Virginia.  Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s for much of the western lowlands. ||In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto a house and a vehicle along Beechwood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level disturbance in the winds aloft was approaching from the west.  As moisture and instability increased, convection formed during the late afternoon into the early evening.||Within a large group of showers and thunderstorms, a short line oriented itself along the wind flow, from southwest to northeast in eastern Boone County.  This caused some repetitive showers to cause some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local downpour occurred over the Pond Fork Valley near Wharton.  A citizen reported 3 inches of rain in 1 hour in their private rain gauge.  Radar estimated a small area of 2 to 2.5 inches.  Ditch lines and runs coming off the steep mountain slopes overflowed after some culverts got clogged with debris.  The overflow collected in poor drainage areas.  A few yards were flooded, along with the local baseball field.  Water also drained into the Wharton Barrett Volunteer Fire Department from their parking lot. ||The streams, including Pond Fork, remained within their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 30th, a convective line of showers formed along a pre-frontal surface trough and intensified as it moved east at 50 MPH. While only isolated thunderstorms occurred, the line produced convective wind damage across portions of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. The highest measured wind gusts were 67 MPH at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport in Latrobe, and 62 MPH at the Wheeling, WV airport. Heavy rainfall with this system caused some flooding of roadways over eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure lifted north through the Chicago and the Lake Michigan vicinity during the late afternoon on Sunday the 17th.  A strong low level wind field existed in the Ohio Valley.  Extension clouds and showers fell during the day on Sunday well ahead of the associated cold front.  Surface surface dew points raised into the upper 50 degrees by nightfall on Sunday evening.  Dew points peaked at 60 degrees in southern West Virginia. ||As the parent low pressure center lifted north toward Ontario, the associated front swept into West Virginia during the night.  Narrow bands of convective showers preceded the front.  The showers were moving at 50 to 60 mph.  Widespread wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph occurred.  A few locations had severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down along Lower Five Mile Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtropical ridge was over the Mid Atlantic with northwesterly flow leading to hot and humid conditions.  This led to a unstable atmosphere and thunderstorms formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree knocked down onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the humidity of a waning heat wave, clusters of showers and thunderstorms starting forming by midday in West Virginia on the 19th.||Their outflow boundaries, along with differential heating and a mid level disturbance, helped develop other areas of showers and thunderstorm into the evening.||Downpours and frequent lightning were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 12 trees about 8 to 10 inches in diameter were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving southeast across the upper Ohio Valley produced thunderstorms, some of which were severe, with some flash flooding as well. A weak and short lived tornado also occurred in Carroll county, Ohio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported Kings Creek road closed due to flash flooding from Kings Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall across western Pennsylvania and the northern West Virginia Panhandle. Flooding of roadways and areas of poor drainage were reported.  Significant flash flooding was reported across portions of Fayette county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported Middle Graves Creek out of its banks and flooding a few homes.
